{"parentUuid":"57550dfb-87c2-4054-9869-525aecd47d92","isSidechain":true,"userType":"external","cwd":"/home/guhaase/projetos/panelbox/papers/17_GraphRAG/desenvolvimento","sessionId":"9bd1c45d-b370-48f7-8b45-37b293556535","version":"2.1.44","gitBranch":"main","agentId":"af5c97b","slug":"validated-doodling-moth","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_013UHqbdwaXf5VApD8PErg8s","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_011rjce3mcp3XBPZrJMToUBt","name":"Write","input":{"file_path":"/home/guhaase/projetos/panelbox/papers/17_GraphRAG/implementacao/data/regulations/basel3_pillar1.md","content":"---
title: "Basel III - Pillar 1: Minimum Capital Requirements"
source: "Bank for International Settlements"
year: 2023
type: regulation
jurisdiction: international
regulatory_body: "Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)"
document_id: "BaselIII_Pillar1"
---

## Overview

Basel III is the third major iteration of the international regulatory framework developed by the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS), housed within the Bank for International Settlements (BIS). The framework was introduced in response to the 2007-2009 global financial crisis, which exposed critical weaknesses in bank capital adequacy, liquidity management, and risk governance. The Basel III reforms were first published in December 2010 and subsequently revised through a series of amendments, most notably the finalised reforms published in December 2017 under BCBS d424, commonly referred to as "Basel III: Finalising post-crisis reforms."

The evolution from Basel I (1988) to Basel II (2004) to Basel III represents a progressive strengthening of prudential standards. Basel I introduced the concept of risk-weighted assets and an 8% minimum capital ratio. Basel II refined risk measurement by introducing three pillars: minimum capital requirements (Pillar 1), supervisory review (Pillar 2), and market discipline (Pillar 3). Basel III significantly raised the quality and quantity of regulatory capital, introduced macroprudential buffers, established a leverage ratio backstop, and reformed the approaches for measuring credit risk, market risk, and operational risk.

Pillar 1 of Basel III specifies the minimum capital requirements that banks must hold against credit risk, market risk, and operational risk. These requirements are expressed as ratios of regulatory capital to risk-weighted assets (RWA). The objective is to ensure that banks maintain sufficient loss-absorbing capacity to withstand financial stress while continuing to support the real economy. Full implementation of the finalised Basel III standards is scheduled for January 2028, following a phased transition period.

## Capital Definition

Under Basel III, regulatory capital is divided into three tiers based on loss-absorbing quality. Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital represents the highest quality of capital and consists of common shares, retained earnings, accumulated other comprehensive income, and other disclosed reserves. CET1 capital must be fully available to absorb losses on a going-concern basis.

Additional Tier 1 (AT1) capital includes instruments such as contingent convertible bonds (CoCos) and other perpetual non-cumulative instruments that can absorb losses through conversion to equity or write-down upon the occurrence of a trigger event. AT1 instruments must have no maturity date and the issuer must have full discretion to cancel coupon payments.

Tier 2 capital encompasses subordinated debt with an original maturity of at least five years, general loan-loss provisions (up to 1.25% of credit risk-weighted assets under the standardised approach), and other instruments that provide loss absorption in a gone-concern scenario. Tier 2 instruments are subject to amortisation in their final five years before maturity.

Basel III imposes rigorous regulatory adjustments and deductions from capital. These include deductions for goodwill and other intangible assets, deferred tax assets arising from temporary differences, investments in own shares, reciprocal cross-holdings, investments in the capital of banking and financial entities outside the scope of regulatory consolidation, and shortfalls in provisions relative to expected losses under the IRB approach. These deductions are primarily applied to CET1 capital, thereby ensuring that reported capital ratios reflect genuinely loss-absorbing resources.

## Minimum Capital Requirements

The Basel III framework establishes three minimum capital ratios that all internationally active banks must satisfy at all times. The minimum CET1 capital ratio is 4.5% of risk-weighted assets. The minimum Tier 1 capital ratio, which comprises CET1 plus AT1 capital, is 6.0% of risk-weighted assets. The minimum total capital ratio, which includes Tier 1 plus Tier 2 capital, is 8.0% of risk-weighted assets.

These minimum ratios represent a significant increase in both the quantity and quality of capital relative to Basel II. Under Basel II, the minimum Tier 1 ratio was 4.0%, and there was no explicit CET1 requirement. The Basel III CET1 requirement of 4.5% alone exceeds the prior Tier 1 minimum, reflecting the post-crisis consensus that common equity is the primary form of loss absorption.

When combined with the capital conservation buffer of 2.5%, the effective minimum CET1 ratio becomes 7.0%, and the effective minimum total capital ratio becomes 10.5%. Banks that fail to meet the combined buffer requirements face automatic restrictions on discretionary distributions, including dividends, share buybacks, and bonus payments, under the maximum distributable amount (MDA) framework.

## Capital Buffers

The capital conservation buffer (CCB) of 2.5% of RWA, composed entirely of CET1 capital, sits above the minimum capital requirements. Introduced under BCBS d189, its purpose is to ensure that banks accumulate capital during normal economic conditions that can be drawn down during periods of stress. When a bank's CET1 ratio falls within the buffer range, progressively stricter constraints are placed on earnings distributions.

The countercyclical capital buffer (CCyB) ranges from 0% to 2.5% of RWA and is also composed of CET1 capital. National authorities set the CCyB rate for their jurisdiction based on macroeconomic and financial conditions, particularly the credit-to-GDP gap. When excessive credit growth signals the build-up of systemic risk, authorities may activate or increase the buffer. Conversely, the buffer may be released during downturns to encourage continued lending.

Global systemically important banks (G-SIBs) are subject to additional capital surcharges ranging from 1.0% to 3.5% of RWA, depending on the bank's systemic importance score. The G-SIB assessment methodology, described in BCBS d296, evaluates banks across five categories: size, interconnectedness, substitutability, complexity, and cross-jurisdictional activity. As of 2023, no bank has been assigned to the highest surcharge bucket. Domestic systemically important banks (D-SIBs) may be subject to additional surcharges set by national authorities, typically ranging from 0.5% to 2.0%.

## Credit Risk - Standardised Approach

The standardised approach for credit risk assigns risk weights to exposures based on external credit ratings and asset class categories. Under the revised standardised approach published in BCBS d424, sovereign exposures to AAA-rated governments receive a 0% risk weight, while exposures to governments rated below B- receive a 150% risk weight. Unrated sovereigns are assigned risk weights based on national discretion.

Bank exposures are risk-weighted according to the external credit rating of the counterparty bank, with risk weights ranging from 20% for AAA to AA- rated banks, to 150% for banks rated below B-. The revised framework eliminates the option to use the sovereign rating as a proxy for bank risk weights in certain cases, introducing instead a standardised credit risk assessment approach (SCRA) for unrated bank exposures, with risk weights of 40% for Grade A and 75% for Grade B banks.

Corporate exposures receive risk weights from 20% (AAA to AA-) to 150% (below BB-), with unrated corporate exposures generally assigned a 100% risk weight. The revised framework introduces more granular risk weights for specialised lending exposures, including project finance, object finance, and commodity finance, with risk weights ranging from 80% to 130% depending on the phase and quality of the project.

Residential mortgage exposures benefit from preferential risk weights. Under the revised standardised approach, risk weights depend on the loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, ranging from 20% for LTV up to 50%, to 70% for LTV between 90% and 100%. Loans that are materially dependent on cash flows generated by the property are treated more conservatively. Retail exposures that meet specific granularity and diversification criteria are assigned a 75% risk weight.

## Credit Risk - Internal Ratings-Based (IRB) Approach

The IRB approach allows banks to use their internal models to estimate key risk parameters for calculating regulatory capital. Under the Foundation IRB (F-IRB) approach, banks estimate the probability of default (PD) for each borrower, while the loss given default (LGD), exposure at default (EAD), and maturity (M) are prescribed by the supervisor. Under the Advanced IRB (A-IRB) approach, banks estimate all four parameters internally, subject to regulatory floors and supervisory validation.

The risk weight function for corporate, sovereign, and bank exposures under the IRB approach is based on the asymptotic single-risk-factor (ASRF) model. The capital requirement (K) for a given exposure is derived from the conditional PD under a stressed scenario, the LGD, and a maturity adjustment factor. The key formula involves the computation of a correlation parameter (R), which varies by asset class and PD level, and the application of the inverse normal distribution to translate PD into a stressed default probability at the 99.9% confidence level.

The Basel III finalised reforms introduced significant constraints on the use of IRB approaches. As published in BCBS d424, the A-IRB approach is no longer available for exposures to large and mid-sized corporates (consolidated revenues exceeding EUR 500 million), banks, and other financial institutions. For these portfolios, banks must use either the F-IRB approach or the standardised approach. Additionally, input floors have been set for key risk parameters: a PD floor of 5 basis points (0.05%) for corporate exposures, LGD floors of 25% for unsecured corporate exposures and 10% for secured exposures (with variations by collateral type), and an EAD floor equal to the sum of on-balance-sheet exposures and 50% of off-balance-sheet exposures using applicable credit conversion factors.

## Market Risk - Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB)

The Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB), published as BCBS d457 in January 2019 (revised minimum capital requirements for market risk), represents a comprehensive overhaul of the market risk framework. The FRTB introduces a clearer boundary between the trading book and the banking book, reducing opportunities for regulatory arbitrage. Banks must document their intent to trade and meet strict criteria for instruments held in the trading book, with supervisory powers to reassign positions.

The revised standardised approach (SA) under FRTB is based on a sensitivities-based method (SbM) that captures delta, vega, and curvature risks. Risk charges are computed for seven risk classes: general interest rate risk (GIRR), credit spread risk for non-securitisations, credit spread risk for securitisations (CTP and non-CTP), equity risk, commodity risk, and foreign exchange risk. The SA also includes a default risk charge (DRC) and a residual risk add-on (RRAO) for instruments with exotic payoffs.

The internal models approach (IMA) under FRTB replaces value-at-risk (VaR) with expected shortfall (ES) as the primary risk measure, calibrated to a 97.5% confidence level over a variable liquidity horizon. The IMA requires banks to pass both backtesting and profit-and-loss attribution (P&L attribution) tests at the desk level. Desks that fail these tests are reverted to the standardised approach, enforcing discipline in model quality and accuracy.

## Operational Risk - Standardised Measurement Approach (SMA)

Basel III replaces all prior approaches to operational risk capital (including the Basic Indicator Approach, the Standardised Approach, and the Advanced Measurement Approaches) with a single Standardised Measurement Approach (SMA), as specified in BCBS d424. The SMA eliminates the use of internal models for operational risk capital, addressing concerns about the comparability and reliability of modelled operational risk estimates.

The SMA calculates operational risk capital based on two components: the Business Indicator Component (BIC) and the Internal Loss Multiplier (ILM). The Business Indicator (BI) is a financial-statement-based proxy for operational risk exposure, computed as the sum of three components: the interest, leases, and dividends component (ILDC), the services component (SC), and the financial component (FC). The BI is then mapped to a marginal coefficient structure that increases progressively across three buckets, producing the BIC.

The Internal Loss Multiplier incorporates a bank's historical loss experience (average annual operational risk losses over a 10-year period) into the capital calculation. Banks with higher historical losses relative to their BIC will face a multiplier above 1.0, while banks with lower loss experience may benefit from a multiplier below 1.0. National supervisors retain the discretion to set the ILM equal to 1.0 for all banks in their jurisdiction, effectively neutralising the impact of internal loss data.

## Leverage Ratio

The Basel III leverage ratio is a non-risk-based backstop measure designed to constrain the build-up of excessive leverage in the banking system. The minimum leverage ratio is set at 3% of Tier 1 capital to the total leverage exposure measure. G-SIBs are subject to an additional leverage ratio buffer equal to 50% of their risk-weighted G-SIB surcharge, which must be met with CET1 capital.

The leverage exposure measure encompasses on-balance-sheet exposures, derivative exposures (measured using SA-CCR), securities financing transaction (SFT) exposures, and off-balance-sheet items converted using specific credit conversion factors. Unlike the risk-based capital framework, the leverage ratio does not differentiate between exposures based on their riskiness, thereby serving as a safeguard against model risk and risk-weight optimisation.

The leverage ratio was introduced in BCBS d270 (revised in BCBS d424) to address a critical lesson of the financial crisis: several large banks that appeared well-capitalised on a risk-weighted basis had, in fact, built up excessive leverage through on- and off-balance-sheet exposures. The leverage ratio provides a simple, transparent, and comparable measure that complements the risk-based capital requirements.

## Output Floor

The output floor, introduced as a key component of the Basel III finalised reforms in BCBS d424, sets a lower bound on the capital requirements calculated using internal models. Specifically, the aggregate risk-weighted assets calculated using internal models-based approaches cannot be lower than 72.5% of the RWA that would result from applying the revised standardised approaches across all risk categories (credit risk, market risk, and operational risk).

The output floor is designed to address the excessive variability in risk-weighted assets observed across banks using internal models, and to ensure a minimum level of conservatism. The floor is being phased in gradually: 50% from January 2023, increasing by 5 percentage points annually, reaching the full 72.5% by January 2028. During the transition period, national supervisors may apply a cap on the increase in total RWA resulting from the output floor.

The output floor applies at the consolidated level and is calculated as the maximum of (a) total RWA computed using the bank's approved approaches, and (b) 72.5% of total RWA computed using only standardised approaches. This mechanism effectively limits the capital benefit that banks can derive from using internal models, promoting greater consistency and comparability across the banking sector.

## Risk-Weighted Assets (RWA)

Total risk-weighted assets represent the denominator in the capital adequacy ratio calculation and comprise the sum of RWA for credit risk, market risk, and operational risk. For credit risk, RWA equals the exposure amount multiplied by the applicable risk weight under the standardised approach, or is derived from the IRB capital requirement formula (K multiplied by 12.5 multiplied by EAD) under the IRB approach.

Market risk RWA is calculated as 12.5 multiplied by the market risk capital charge derived under either the FRTB standardised approach or the FRTB internal models approach. Operational risk RWA equals 12.5 multiplied by the operational risk capital charge computed under the SMA. The factor of 12.5 (the reciprocal of the 8% minimum total capital ratio) converts capital charges into RWA-equivalent amounts.

The calculation of total RWA is subject to the output floor, which ensures that total RWA does not fall below 72.5% of what would result from exclusive use of standardised approaches. Additionally, credit valuation adjustment (CVA) risk charges, calculated under either the basic approach (BA-CVA) or the standardised approach (SA-CVA) as specified in BCBS d424, contribute to total RWA and capture the risk of mark-to-market losses on derivatives due to deterioration in counterparty creditworthiness.

## Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR)

The Standardised Approach for Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR), introduced in BCBS d279 and incorporated into the Basel III finalised framework, replaces the prior Current Exposure Method (CEM) and Standardised Method (SM) for measuring counterparty credit risk exposures arising from OTC derivatives, exchange-traded derivatives, and long-settlement transactions. SA-CCR provides a more risk-sensitive framework that differentiates between margined and unmargined transactions.

The SA-CCR exposure at default is calculated as: EAD = alpha multiplied by (Replacement Cost + Potential Future Exposure), where alpha is set at 1.4. Replacement Cost (RC) reflects the current mark-to-market exposure, considering the value of collateral and netting agreements. For margined trades, RC also incorporates the minimum transfer amount and the threshold. Potential Future Exposure (PFE) is computed as a multiplier (which reduces PFE when the portfolio is over-collateralised or out-of-the-money) applied to an aggregate add-on across five asset classes: interest rates, foreign exchange, credit, equity, and commodity.

The SA-CCR framework recognises the risk-reducing benefits of netting agreements and margin arrangements more accurately than prior methods. Within each asset class, hedging sets allow for the partial or full offsetting of long and short positions. The framework applies supervisory-determined parameters including supervisory delta adjustments, supervisory volatilities, and supervisory correlation parameters, ensuring consistency and comparability across banks. SA-CCR outputs feed into both the risk-based capital framework (as EAD inputs to credit risk RWA calculations) and the leverage ratio exposure measure.

document_id: "BaselIII_Pillar1"
---

## Overview

Basel III is the third major iteration of the international regulatory framework developed by the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS), housed within the Bank for International Settlements (BIS). The framework was introduced in response to the 2007-2009 global financial crisis, which exposed critical weaknesses in bank capital adequacy, liquidity management, and risk governance. The Basel III reforms were first published in December 2010 and subsequently revised through a series of amendments, most notably the finalised reforms published in December 2017 under BCBS d424, commonly referred to as "Basel III: Finalising post-crisis reforms."

The evolution from Basel I (1988) to Basel II (2004) to Basel III represents a progressive strengthening of prudential standards. Basel I introduced the concept of risk-weighted assets and an 8% minimum capital ratio. Basel II refined risk measurement by introducing three pillars: minimum capital requirements (Pillar 1), supervisory review (Pillar 2), and market discipline (Pillar 3). Basel III significantly raised the quality and quantity of regulatory capital, introduced macroprudential buffers, established a leverage ratio backstop, and reformed the approaches for measuring credit risk, market risk, and operational risk.

Pillar 1 of Basel III specifies the minimum capital requirements that banks must hold against credit risk, market risk, and operational risk. These requirements are expressed as ratios of regulatory capital to risk-weighted assets (RWA). The objective is to ensure that banks maintain sufficient loss-absorbing capacity to withstand financial stress while continuing to support the real economy. Full implementation of the finalised Basel III standards is scheduled for January 2028, following a phased transition period.

## Capital Definition

Under Basel III, regulatory capital is divided into three tiers based on loss-absorbing quality. Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital represents the highest quality of capital and consists of common shares, retained earnings, accumulated other comprehensive income, and other disclosed reserves. CET1 capital must be fully available to absorb losses on a going-concern basis.

Additional Tier 1 (AT1) capital includes instruments such as contingent convertible bonds (CoCos) and other perpetual non-cumulative instruments that can absorb losses through conversion to equity or write-down upon the occurrence of a trigger event. AT1 instruments must have no maturity date and the issuer must have full discretion to cancel coupon payments.

Tier 2 capital encompasses subordinated debt with an original maturity of at least five years, general loan-loss provisions (up to 1.25% of credit risk-weighted assets under the standardised approach), and other instruments that provide loss absorption in a gone-concern scenario. Tier 2 instruments are subject to amortisation in their final five years before maturity.

Basel III imposes rigorous regulatory adjustments and deductions from capital. These include deductions for goodwill and other intangible assets, deferred tax assets arising from temporary differences, investments in own shares, reciprocal cross-holdings, investments in the capital of banking and financial entities outside the scope of regulatory consolidation, and shortfalls in provisions relative to expected losses under the IRB approach. These deductions are primarily applied to CET1 capital, thereby ensuring that reported capital ratios reflect genuinely loss-absorbing resources.

## Minimum Capital Requirements

The Basel III framework establishes three minimum capital ratios that all internationally active banks must satisfy at all times. The minimum CET1 capital ratio is 4.5% of risk-weighted assets. The minimum Tier 1 capital ratio, which comprises CET1 plus AT1 capital, is 6.0% of risk-weighted assets. The minimum total capital ratio, which includes Tier 1 plus Tier 2 capital, is 8.0% of risk-weighted assets.

These minimum ratios represent a significant increase in both the quantity and quality of capital relative to Basel II. Under Basel II, the minimum Tier 1 ratio was 4.0%, and there was no explicit CET1 requirement. The Basel III CET1 requirement of 4.5% alone exceeds the prior Tier 1 minimum, reflecting the post-crisis consensus that common equity is the primary form of loss absorption.

When combined with the capital conservation buffer of 2.5%, the effective minimum CET1 ratio becomes 7.0%, and the effective minimum total capital ratio becomes 10.5%. Banks that fail to meet the combined buffer requirements face automatic restrictions on discretionary distributions, including dividends, share buybacks, and bonus payments, under the maximum distributable amount (MDA) framework.

## Capital Buffers

The capital conservation buffer (CCB) of 2.5% of RWA, composed entirely of CET1 capital, sits above the minimum capital requirements. Introduced under BCBS d189, its purpose is to ensure that banks accumulate capital during normal economic conditions that can be drawn down during periods of stress. When a bank's CET1 ratio falls within the buffer range, progressively stricter constraints are placed on earnings distributions.

The countercyclical capital buffer (CCyB) ranges from 0% to 2.5% of RWA and is also composed of CET1 capital. National authorities set the CCyB rate for their jurisdiction based on macroeconomic and financial conditions, particularly the credit-to-GDP gap. When excessive credit growth signals the build-up of systemic risk, authorities may activate or increase the buffer. Conversely, the buffer may be released during downturns to encourage continued lending.

Global systemically important banks (G-SIBs) are subject to additional capital surcharges ranging from 1.0% to 3.5% of RWA, depending on the bank's systemic importance score. The G-SIB assessment methodology, described in BCBS d296, evaluates banks across five categories: size, interconnectedness, substitutability, complexity, and cross-jurisdictional activity. As of 2023, no bank has been assigned to the highest surcharge bucket. Domestic systemically important banks (D-SIBs) may be subject to additional surcharges set by national authorities, typically ranging from 0.5% to 2.0%.

## Credit Risk - Standardised Approach

The standardised approach for credit risk assigns risk weights to exposures based on external credit ratings and asset class categories. Under the revised standardised approach published in BCBS d424, sovereign exposures to AAA-rated governments receive a 0% risk weight, while exposures to governments rated below B- receive a 150% risk weight. Unrated sovereigns are assigned risk weights based on national discretion.

Bank exposures are risk-weighted according to the external credit rating of the counterparty bank, with risk weights ranging from 20% for AAA to AA- rated banks, to 150% for banks rated below B-. The revised framework eliminates the option to use the sovereign rating as a proxy for bank risk weights in certain cases, introducing instead a standardised credit risk assessment approach (SCRA) for unrated bank exposures, with risk weights of 40% for Grade A and 75% for Grade B banks.

Corporate exposures receive risk weights from 20% (AAA to AA-) to 150% (below BB-), with unrated corporate exposures generally assigned a 100% risk weight. The revised framework introduces more granular risk weights for specialised lending exposures, including project finance, object finance, and commodity finance, with risk weights ranging from 80% to 130% depending on the phase and quality of the project.

Residential mortgage exposures benefit from preferential risk weights. Under the revised standardised approach, risk weights depend on the loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, ranging from 20% for LTV up to 50%, to 70% for LTV between 90% and 100%. Loans that are materially dependent on cash flows generated by the property are treated more conservatively. Retail exposures that meet specific granularity and diversification criteria are assigned a 75% risk weight.

## Credit Risk - Internal Ratings-Based (IRB) Approach

The IRB approach allows banks to use their internal models to estimate key risk parameters for calculating regulatory capital. Under the Foundation IRB (F-IRB) approach, banks estimate the probability of default (PD) for each borrower, while the loss given default (LGD), exposure at default (EAD), and maturity (M) are prescribed by the supervisor. Under the Advanced IRB (A-IRB) approach, banks estimate all four parameters internally, subject to regulatory floors and supervisory validation.

The risk weight function for corporate, sovereign, and bank exposures under the IRB approach is based on the asymptotic single-risk-factor (ASRF) model. The capital requirement (K) for a given exposure is derived from the conditional PD under a stressed scenario, the LGD, and a maturity adjustment factor. The key formula involves the computation of a correlation parameter (R), which varies by asset class and PD level, and the application of the inverse normal distribution to translate PD into a stressed default probability at the 99.9% confidence level.

The Basel III finalised reforms introduced significant constraints on the use of IRB approaches. As published in BCBS d424, the A-IRB approach is no longer available for exposures to large and mid-sized corporates (consolidated revenues exceeding EUR 500 million), banks, and other financial institutions. For these portfolios, banks must use either the F-IRB approach or the standardised approach. Additionally, input floors have been set for key risk parameters: a PD floor of 5 basis points (0.05%) for corporate exposures, LGD floors of 25% for unsecured corporate exposures and 10% for secured exposures (with variations by collateral type), and an EAD floor equal to the sum of on-balance-sheet exposures and 50% of off-balance-sheet exposures using applicable credit conversion factors.

## Market Risk - Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB)

The Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB), published as BCBS d457 in January 2019 (revised minimum capital requirements for market risk), represents a comprehensive overhaul of the market risk framework. The FRTB introduces a clearer boundary between the trading book and the banking book, reducing opportunities for regulatory arbitrage. Banks must document their intent to trade and meet strict criteria for instruments held in the trading book, with supervisory powers to reassign positions.

The revised standardised approach (SA) under FRTB is based on a sensitivities-based method (SbM) that captures delta, vega, and curvature risks. Risk charges are computed for seven risk classes: general interest rate risk (GIRR), credit spread risk for non-securitisations, credit spread risk for securitisations (CTP and non-CTP), equity risk, commodity risk, and foreign exchange risk. The SA also includes a default risk charge (DRC) and a residual risk add-on (RRAO) for instruments with exotic payoffs.

The internal models approach (IMA) under FRTB replaces value-at-risk (VaR) with expected shortfall (ES) as the primary risk measure, calibrated to a 97.5% confidence level over a variable liquidity horizon. The IMA requires banks to pass both backtesting and profit-and-loss attribution (P&L attribution) tests at the desk level. Desks that fail these tests are reverted to the standardised approach, enforcing discipline in model quality and accuracy.

## Operational Risk - Standardised Measurement Approach (SMA)

Basel III replaces all prior approaches to operational risk capital (including the Basic Indicator Approach, the Standardised Approach, and the Advanced Measurement Approaches) with a single Standardised Measurement Approach (SMA), as specified in BCBS d424. The SMA eliminates the use of internal models for operational risk capital, addressing concerns about the comparability and reliability of modelled operational risk estimates.

The SMA calculates operational risk capital based on two components: the Business Indicator Component (BIC) and the Internal Loss Multiplier (ILM). The Business Indicator (BI) is a financial-statement-based proxy for operational risk exposure, computed as the sum of three components: the interest, leases, and dividends component (ILDC), the services component (SC), and the financial component (FC). The BI is then mapped to a marginal coefficient structure that increases progressively across three buckets, producing the BIC.

The Internal Loss Multiplier incorporates a bank's historical loss experience (average annual operational risk losses over a 10-year period) into the capital calculation. Banks with higher historical losses relative to their BIC will face a multiplier above 1.0, while banks with lower loss experience may benefit from a multiplier below 1.0. National supervisors retain the discretion to set the ILM equal to 1.0 for all banks in their jurisdiction, effectively neutralising the impact of internal loss data.

## Leverage Ratio

The Basel III leverage ratio is a non-risk-based backstop measure designed to constrain the build-up of excessive leverage in the banking system. The minimum leverage ratio is set at 3% of Tier 1 capital to the total leverage exposure measure. G-SIBs are subject to an additional leverage ratio buffer equal to 50% of their risk-weighted G-SIB surcharge, which must be met with CET1 capital.

The leverage exposure measure encompasses on-balance-sheet exposures, derivative exposures (measured using SA-CCR), securities financing transaction (SFT) exposures, and off-balance-sheet items converted using specific credit conversion factors. Unlike the risk-based capital framework, the leverage ratio does not differentiate between exposures based on their riskiness, thereby serving as a safeguard against model risk and risk-weight optimisation.

The leverage ratio was introduced in BCBS d270 (revised in BCBS d424) to address a critical lesson of the financial crisis: several large banks that appeared well-capitalised on a risk-weighted basis had, in fact, built up excessive leverage through on- and off-balance-sheet exposures. The leverage ratio provides a simple, transparent, and comparable measure that complements the risk-based capital requirements.

## Output Floor

The output floor, introduced as a key component of the Basel III finalised reforms in BCBS d424, sets a lower bound on the capital requirements calculated using internal models. Specifically, the aggregate risk-weighted assets calculated using internal models-based approaches cannot be lower than 72.5% of the RWA that would result from applying the revised standardised approaches across all risk categories (credit risk, market risk, and operational risk).

The output floor is designed to address the excessive variability in risk-weighted assets observed across banks using internal models, and to ensure a minimum level of conservatism. The floor is being phased in gradually: 50% from January 2023, increasing by 5 percentage points annually, reaching the full 72.5% by January 2028. During the transition period, national supervisors may apply a cap on the increase in total RWA resulting from the output floor.

The output floor applies at the consolidated level and is calculated as the maximum of (a) total RWA computed using the bank's approved approaches, and (b) 72.5% of total RWA computed using only standardised approaches. This mechanism effectively limits the capital benefit that banks can derive from using internal models, promoting greater consistency and comparability across the banking sector.

## Risk-Weighted Assets (RWA)

Total risk-weighted assets represent the denominator in the capital adequacy ratio calculation and comprise the sum of RWA for credit risk, market risk, and operational risk. For credit risk, RWA equals the exposure amount multiplied by the applicable risk weight under the standardised approach, or is derived from the IRB capital requirement formula (K multiplied by 12.5 multiplied by EAD) under the IRB approach.

Market risk RWA is calculated as 12.5 multiplied by the market risk capital charge derived under either the FRTB standardised approach or the FRTB internal models approach. Operational risk RWA equals 12.5 multiplied by the operational risk capital charge computed under the SMA. The factor of 12.5 (the reciprocal of the 8% minimum total capital ratio) converts capital charges into RWA-equivalent amounts.

The calculation of total RWA is subject to the output floor, which ensures that total RWA does not fall below 72.5% of what would result from exclusive use of standardised approaches. Additionally, credit valuation adjustment (CVA) risk charges, calculated under either the basic approach (BA-CVA) or the standardised approach (SA-CVA) as specified in BCBS d424, contribute to total RWA and capture the risk of mark-to-market losses on derivatives due to deterioration in counterparty creditworthiness.

## Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR)

The Standardised Approach for Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR), introduced in BCBS d279 and incorporated into the Basel III finalised framework, replaces the prior Current Exposure Method (CEM) and Standardised Method (SM) for measuring counterparty credit risk exposures arising from OTC derivatives, exchange-traded derivatives, and long-settlement transactions. SA-CCR provides a more risk-sensitive framework that differentiates between margined and unmargined transactions.

The SA-CCR exposure at default is calculated as: EAD = alpha multiplied by (Replacement Cost + Potential Future Exposure), where alpha is set at 1.4. Replacement Cost (RC) reflects the current mark-to-market exposure, considering the value of collateral and netting agreements. For margined trades, RC also incorporates the minimum transfer amount and the threshold. Potential Future Exposure (PFE) is computed as a multiplier (which reduces PFE when the portfolio is over-collateralised or out-of-the-money) applied to an aggregate add-on across five asset classes: interest rates, foreign exchange, credit, equity, and commodity.

The SA-CCR framework recognises the risk-reducing benefits of netting agreements and margin arrangements more accurately than prior methods. Within each asset class, hedging sets allow for the partial or full offsetting of long and short positions. The framework applies supervisory-determined parameters including supervisory delta adjustments, supervisory volatilities, and supervisory correlation parameters, ensuring consistency and comparability across banks. SA-CCR outputs feed into both the risk-based capital framework (as EAD inputs to credit risk RWA calculations) and the leverage ratio exposure measure.

year: 2023
type: regulation

  8. Operational Risk (Standardised Measurement Approach - SMA, replacing AMA)
  9. Leverage Ratio (3% minimum, calculation methodology)
  10. Output Floor (72.5% of standardised approaches)
  11. Risk-Weighted Assets (RWA calculation overview)
  12. Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR)

- Include specific numerical thresholds, percentages, and formulas where relevant

- Each section should have 3-8 paragraphs to be rich enough for triple extraction
- Target: ~2000-2500 words total
- Do NOT use emojis

title: "Basel III - Pillar 1: Minimum Capital Requirements"
source: "Bank for International Settlements"
year: 2023
type: regulation
jurisdiction: international
regulatory_body: "Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)"

## Overview

Basel III is the third major iteration of the international regulatory framework developed by the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS), housed within the Bank for International Settlements (BIS). The framework was introduced in response to the 2007-2009 global financial crisis, which exposed critical weaknesses in bank capital adequacy, liquidity management, and risk governance. The Basel III reforms were first published in December 2010 and subsequently revised through a series of amendments, most notably the finalised reforms published in December 2017 under BCBS d424, commonly referred to as "Basel III: Finalising post-crisis reforms."

The evolution from Basel I (1988) to Basel II (2004) to Basel III represents a progressive strengthening of prudential standards. Basel I introduced the concept of risk-weighted assets and an 8% minimum capital ratio. Basel II refined risk measurement by introducing three pillars: minimum capital requirements (Pillar 1), supervisory review (Pillar 2), and market discipline (Pillar 3). Basel III significantly raised the quality and quantity of regulatory capital, introduced macroprudential buffers, established a leverage ratio backstop, and reformed the approaches for measuring credit risk, market risk, and operational risk.

Pillar 1 of Basel III specifies the minimum capital requirements that banks must hold against credit risk, market risk, and operational risk. These requirements are expressed as ratios of regulatory capital to risk-weighted assets (RWA). The objective is to ensure that banks maintain sufficient loss-absorbing capacity to withstand financial stress while continuing to support the real economy. Full implementation of the finalised Basel III standards is scheduled for January 2028, following a phased transition period.

## Capital Definition

Under Basel III, regulatory capital is divided into three tiers based on loss-absorbing quality. Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital represents the highest quality of capital and consists of common shares, retained earnings, accumulated other comprehensive income, and other disclosed reserves. CET1 capital must be fully available to absorb losses on a going-concern basis.

Additional Tier 1 (AT1) capital includes instruments such as contingent convertible bonds (CoCos) and other perpetual non-cumulative instruments that can absorb losses through conversion to equity or write-down upon the occurrence of a trigger event. AT1 instruments must have no maturity date and the issuer must have full discretion to cancel coupon payments.

Tier 2 capital encompasses subordinated debt with an original maturity of at least five years, general loan-loss provisions (up to 1.25% of credit risk-weighted assets under the standardised approach), and other instruments that provide loss absorption in a gone-concern scenario. Tier 2 instruments are subject to amortisation in their final five years before maturity.

Basel III imposes rigorous regulatory adjustments and deductions from capital. These include deductions for goodwill and other intangible assets, deferred tax assets arising from temporary differences, investments in own shares, reciprocal cross-holdings, investments in the capital of banking and financial entities outside the scope of regulatory consolidation, and shortfalls in provisions relative to expected losses under the IRB approach. These deductions are primarily applied to CET1 capital, thereby ensuring that reported capital ratios reflect genuinely loss-absorbing resources.

## Minimum Capital Requirements

The Basel III framework establishes three minimum capital ratios that all internationally active banks must satisfy at all times. The minimum CET1 capital ratio is 4.5% of risk-weighted assets. The minimum Tier 1 capital ratio, which comprises CET1 plus AT1 capital, is 6.0% of risk-weighted assets. The minimum total capital ratio, which includes Tier 1 plus Tier 2 capital, is 8.0% of risk-weighted assets.

These minimum ratios represent a significant increase in both the quantity and quality of capital relative to Basel II. Under Basel II, the minimum Tier 1 ratio was 4.0%, and there was no explicit CET1 requirement. The Basel III CET1 requirement of 4.5% alone exceeds the prior Tier 1 minimum, reflecting the post-crisis consensus that common equity is the primary form of loss absorption.

When combined with the capital conservation buffer of 2.5%, the effective minimum CET1 ratio becomes 7.0%, and the effective minimum total capital ratio becomes 10.5%. Banks that fail to meet the combined buffer requirements face automatic restrictions on discretionary distributions, including dividends, share buybacks, and bonus payments, under the maximum distributable amount (MDA) framework.

## Capital Buffers

The capital conservation buffer (CCB) of 2.5% of RWA, composed entirely of CET1 capital, sits above the minimum capital requirements. Introduced under BCBS d189, its purpose is to ensure that banks accumulate capital during normal economic conditions that can be drawn down during periods of stress. When a bank's CET1 ratio falls within the buffer range, progressively stricter constraints are placed on earnings distributions.

The countercyclical capital buffer (CCyB) ranges from 0% to 2.5% of RWA and is also composed of CET1 capital. National authorities set the CCyB rate for their jurisdiction based on macroeconomic and financial conditions, particularly the credit-to-GDP gap. When excessive credit growth signals the build-up of systemic risk, authorities may activate or increase the buffer. Conversely, the buffer may be released during downturns to encourage continued lending.

Global systemically important banks (G-SIBs) are subject to additional capital surcharges ranging from 1.0% to 3.5% of RWA, depending on the bank's systemic importance score. The G-SIB assessment methodology, described in BCBS d296, evaluates banks across five categories: size, interconnectedness, substitutability, complexity, and cross-jurisdictional activity. As of 2023, no bank has been assigned to the highest surcharge bucket. Domestic systemically important banks (D-SIBs) may be subject to additional surcharges set by national authorities, typically ranging from 0.5% to 2.0%.

## Credit Risk - Standardised Approach

The standardised approach for credit risk assigns risk weights to exposures based on external credit ratings and asset class categories. Under the revised standardised approach published in BCBS d424, sovereign exposures to AAA-rated governments receive a 0% risk weight, while exposures to governments rated below B- receive a 150% risk weight. Unrated sovereigns are assigned risk weights based on national discretion.

Bank exposures are risk-weighted according to the external credit rating of the counterparty bank, with risk weights ranging from 20% for AAA to AA- rated banks, to 150% for banks rated below B-. The revised framework eliminates the option to use the sovereign rating as a proxy for bank risk weights in certain cases, introducing instead a standardised credit risk assessment approach (SCRA) for unrated bank exposures, with risk weights of 40% for Grade A and 75% for Grade B banks.

Corporate exposures receive risk weights from 20% (AAA to AA-) to 150% (below BB-), with unrated corporate exposures generally assigned a 100% risk weight. The revised framework introduces more granular risk weights for specialised lending exposures, including project finance, object finance, and commodity finance, with risk weights ranging from 80% to 130% depending on the phase and quality of the project.

Residential mortgage exposures benefit from preferential risk weights. Under the revised standardised approach, risk weights depend on the loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, ranging from 20% for LTV up to 50%, to 70% for LTV between 90% and 100%. Loans that are materially dependent on cash flows generated by the property are treated more conservatively. Retail exposures that meet specific granularity and diversification criteria are assigned a 75% risk weight.

## Credit Risk - Internal Ratings-Based (IRB) Approach

The IRB approach allows banks to use their internal models to estimate key risk parameters for calculating regulatory capital. Under the Foundation IRB (F-IRB) approach, banks estimate the probability of default (PD) for each borrower, while the loss given default (LGD), exposure at default (EAD), and maturity (M) are prescribed by the supervisor. Under the Advanced IRB (A-IRB) approach, banks estimate all four parameters internally, subject to regulatory floors and supervisory validation.

The risk weight function for corporate, sovereign, and bank exposures under the IRB approach is based on the asymptotic single-risk-factor (ASRF) model. The capital requirement (K) for a given exposure is derived from the conditional PD under a stressed scenario, the LGD, and a maturity adjustment factor. The key formula involves the computation of a correlation parameter (R), which varies by asset class and PD level, and the application of the inverse normal distribution to translate PD into a stressed default probability at the 99.9% confidence level.

The Basel III finalised reforms introduced significant constraints on the use of IRB approaches. As published in BCBS d424, the A-IRB approach is no longer available for exposures to large and mid-sized corporates (consolidated revenues exceeding EUR 500 million), banks, and other financial institutions. For these portfolios, banks must use either the F-IRB approach or the standardised approach. Additionally, input floors have been set for key risk parameters: a PD floor of 5 basis points (0.05%) for corporate exposures, LGD floors of 25% for unsecured corporate exposures and 10% for secured exposures (with variations by collateral type), and an EAD floor equal to the sum of on-balance-sheet exposures and 50% of off-balance-sheet exposures using applicable credit conversion factors.

## Market Risk - Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB)

The Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB), published as BCBS d457 in January 2019 (revised minimum capital requirements for market risk), represents a comprehensive overhaul of the market risk framework. The FRTB introduces a clearer boundary between the trading book and the banking book, reducing opportunities for regulatory arbitrage. Banks must document their intent to trade and meet strict criteria for instruments held in the trading book, with supervisory powers to reassign positions.

The revised standardised approach (SA) under FRTB is based on a sensitivities-based method (SbM) that captures delta, vega, and curvature risks. Risk charges are computed for seven risk classes: general interest rate risk (GIRR), credit spread risk for non-securitisations, credit spread risk for securitisations (CTP and non-CTP), equity risk, commodity risk, and foreign exchange risk. The SA also includes a default risk charge (DRC) and a residual risk add-on (RRAO) for instruments with exotic payoffs.

The internal models approach (IMA) under FRTB replaces value-at-risk (VaR) with expected shortfall (ES) as the primary risk measure, calibrated to a 97.5% confidence level over a variable liquidity horizon. The IMA requires banks to pass both backtesting and profit-and-loss attribution (P&L attribution) tests at the desk level. Desks that fail these tests are reverted to the standardised approach, enforcing discipline in model quality and accuracy.

## Operational Risk - Standardised Measurement Approach (SMA)

Basel III replaces all prior approaches to operational risk capital (including the Basic Indicator Approach, the Standardised Approach, and the Advanced Measurement Approaches) with a single Standardised Measurement Approach (SMA), as specified in BCBS d424. The SMA eliminates the use of internal models for operational risk capital, addressing concerns about the comparability and reliability of modelled operational risk estimates.

The SMA calculates operational risk capital based on two components: the Business Indicator Component (BIC) and the Internal Loss Multiplier (ILM). The Business Indicator (BI) is a financial-statement-based proxy for operational risk exposure, computed as the sum of three components: the interest, leases, and dividends component (ILDC), the services component (SC), and the financial component (FC). The BI is then mapped to a marginal coefficient structure that increases progressively across three buckets, producing the BIC.

The Internal Loss Multiplier incorporates a bank's historical loss experience (average annual operational risk losses over a 10-year period) into the capital calculation. Banks with higher historical losses relative to their BIC will face a multiplier above 1.0, while banks with lower loss experience may benefit from a multiplier below 1.0. National supervisors retain the discretion to set the ILM equal to 1.0 for all banks in their jurisdiction, effectively neutralising the impact of internal loss data.

## Leverage Ratio

The Basel III leverage ratio is a non-risk-based backstop measure designed to constrain the build-up of excessive leverage in the banking system. The minimum leverage ratio is set at 3% of Tier 1 capital to the total leverage exposure measure. G-SIBs are subject to an additional leverage ratio buffer equal to 50% of their risk-weighted G-SIB surcharge, which must be met with CET1 capital.

The leverage exposure measure encompasses on-balance-sheet exposures, derivative exposures (measured using SA-CCR), securities financing transaction (SFT) exposures, and off-balance-sheet items converted using specific credit conversion factors. Unlike the risk-based capital framework, the leverage ratio does not differentiate between exposures based on their riskiness, thereby serving as a safeguard against model risk and risk-weight optimisation.

The leverage ratio was introduced in BCBS d270 (revised in BCBS d424) to address a critical lesson of the financial crisis: several large banks that appeared well-capitalised on a risk-weighted basis had, in fact, built up excessive leverage through on- and off-balance-sheet exposures. The leverage ratio provides a simple, transparent, and comparable measure that complements the risk-based capital requirements.

## Output Floor

The output floor, introduced as a key component of the Basel III finalised reforms in BCBS d424, sets a lower bound on the capital requirements calculated using internal models. Specifically, the aggregate risk-weighted assets calculated using internal models-based approaches cannot be lower than 72.5% of the RWA that would result from applying the revised standardised approaches across all risk categories (credit risk, market risk, and operational risk).

The output floor is designed to address the excessive variability in risk-weighted assets observed across banks using internal models, and to ensure a minimum level of conservatism. The floor is being phased in gradually: 50% from January 2023, increasing by 5 percentage points annually, reaching the full 72.5% by January 2028. During the transition period, national supervisors may apply a cap on the increase in total RWA resulting from the output floor.

The output floor applies at the consolidated level and is calculated as the maximum of (a) total RWA computed using the bank's approved approaches, and (b) 72.5% of total RWA computed using only standardised approaches. This mechanism effectively limits the capital benefit that banks can derive from using internal models, promoting greater consistency and comparability across the banking sector.

## Risk-Weighted Assets (RWA)

Total risk-weighted assets represent the denominator in the capital adequacy ratio calculation and comprise the sum of RWA for credit risk, market risk, and operational risk. For credit risk, RWA equals the exposure amount multiplied by the applicable risk weight under the standardised approach, or is derived from the IRB capital requirement formula (K multiplied by 12.5 multiplied by EAD) under the IRB approach.

Market risk RWA is calculated as 12.5 multiplied by the market risk capital charge derived under either the FRTB standardised approach or the FRTB internal models approach. Operational risk RWA equals 12.5 multiplied by the operational risk capital charge computed under the SMA. The factor of 12.5 (the reciprocal of the 8% minimum total capital ratio) converts capital charges into RWA-equivalent amounts.

The calculation of total RWA is subject to the output floor, which ensures that total RWA does not fall below 72.5% of what would result from exclusive use of standardised approaches. Additionally, credit valuation adjustment (CVA) risk charges, calculated under either the basic approach (BA-CVA) or the standardised approach (SA-CVA) as specified in BCBS d424, contribute to total RWA and capture the risk of mark-to-market losses on derivatives due to deterioration in counterparty creditworthiness.

## Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR)

The Standardised Approach for Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR), introduced in BCBS d279 and incorporated into the Basel III finalised framework, replaces the prior Current Exposure Method (CEM) and Standardised Method (SM) for measuring counterparty credit risk exposures arising from OTC derivatives, exchange-traded derivatives, and long-settlement transactions. SA-CCR provides a more risk-sensitive framework that differentiates between margined and unmargined transactions.

The SA-CCR exposure at default is calculated as: EAD = alpha multiplied by (Replacement Cost + Potential Future Exposure), where alpha is set at 1.4. Replacement Cost (RC) reflects the current mark-to-market exposure, considering the value of collateral and netting agreements. For margined trades, RC also incorporates the minimum transfer amount and the threshold. Potential Future Exposure (PFE) is computed as a multiplier (which reduces PFE when the portfolio is over-collateralised or out-of-the-money) applied to an aggregate add-on across five asset classes: interest rates, foreign exchange, credit, equity, and commodity.

The SA-CCR framework recognises the risk-reducing benefits of netting agreements and margin arrangements more accurately than prior methods. Within each asset class, hedging sets allow for the partial or full offsetting of long and short positions. The framework applies supervisory-determined parameters including supervisory delta adjustments, supervisory volatilities, and supervisory correlation parameters, ensuring consistency and comparability across banks. SA-CCR outputs feed into both the risk-based capital framework (as EAD inputs to credit risk RWA calculations) and the leverage ratio exposure measure.

title: "Basel III - Pillar 2: Supervisory Review Process"
source: "Bank for International Settlements"
year: 2023
type: regulation
jurisdiction: international
regulatory_body: "Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)"
document_id: "BaselIII_Pillar2"
---

## Overview and Purpose of Pillar 2

Pillar 2 of the Basel III framework establishes the Supervisory Review Process (SRP), which serves as a critical complement to the minimum capital requirements defined under Pillar 1 and the market discipline mechanisms of Pillar 3. The primary objective of Pillar 2 is to ensure that banks maintain adequate capital to support all material risks in their business, going beyond the regulatory minimums prescribed by Pillar 1. It also encourages banks to develop and employ improved risk management techniques for monitoring and managing their exposures.

The Supervisory Review Process was originally introduced in the Basel II framework and has been substantially reinforced under Basel III following the lessons of the 2007-2009 global financial crisis. The BCBS recognized that minimum capital requirements alone are insufficient to ensure the safety and soundness of individual institutions and the stability of the broader financial system. Pillar 2 addresses this gap by requiring a forward-looking assessment of risks that may not be fully captured by Pillar 1 calculations, including concentration risk, interest rate risk in the banking book, and strategic risk.

Pillar 2 operates through a dialogue between banks and their supervisors. Banks are expected to conduct their own internal assessments of capital adequacy relative to their risk profiles, while supervisors are responsible for evaluating those assessments and taking appropriate action when deficiencies are identified. This dual responsibility creates a framework of shared accountability that strengthens the overall resilience of the banking sector.

The BCBS guidance on Pillar 2, as outlined in the document "Supervisory Review Process" (SRP), establishes that supervisory authorities should intervene at an early stage to prevent capital from falling below minimum levels. Supervisors retain the authority to impose additional capital buffers and to require remedial measures when banks are found to operate with inadequate capital or risk management practices.

## Four Key Principles of Supervisory Review

The Supervisory Review Process is built upon four foundational principles articulated by the BCBS. These principles define the respective responsibilities of banks and supervisors and form the backbone of the Pillar 2 framework.

**Principle 1** states that banks should have a process for assessing their overall capital adequacy in relation to their risk profile and a strategy for maintaining their capital levels. This principle establishes the requirement for the Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP), through which banks must identify, measure, and aggregate all material risks and determine the capital needed to cover those risks under both normal and stressed conditions.

**Principle 2** requires that supervisors should review and evaluate banks' internal capital adequacy assessments and strategies, as well as their ability to monitor and ensure compliance with regulatory capital ratios. Where supervisors are not satisfied with the results of this process, they should take appropriate supervisory action. This includes the Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP).

**Principle 3** establishes that supervisors should expect banks to operate above the minimum regulatory capital ratios and should have the ability to require banks to hold capital in excess of the Pillar 1 minimum. This principle recognizes that Pillar 1 requirements represent a floor, not a ceiling, and that banks with higher risk profiles or weaker risk management should maintain commensurately higher capital levels.

**Principle 4** directs that supervisors should seek to intervene at an early stage to prevent capital from falling below the minimum levels required to support the risk characteristics of a particular bank. Rapid remedial action should be taken if capital is not maintained or restored. Supervisory responses may include intensified monitoring, requiring a capital restoration plan, restricting dividends, or requiring the bank to raise additional capital immediately.

## Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP)

The Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) is the cornerstone of Pillar 2 from the perspective of the banking institution. Under BCBS guidelines, every bank is required to have an ICAAP that is proportionate to the nature, scale, and complexity of its activities. The ICAAP must be a comprehensive, institution-wide process that considers all material risks to which the bank is exposed.

A robust ICAAP encompasses several key elements: identification and measurement of all material risks, including those not covered or not fully captured under Pillar 1; setting of internal capital targets that are consistent with the bank's risk profile, operating environment, and strategic plans; and integration of capital planning with business planning and risk appetite frameworks. The ICAAP must also incorporate forward-looking elements, including stress testing and scenario analysis.

Board oversight and senior management involvement are essential components of the ICAAP. The bank's board of directors bears ultimate responsibility for ensuring that the institution has adequate capital relative to its risks. Senior management must develop the processes for assessing capital adequacy, set appropriate capital targets, and ensure that capital planning is integrated into the institution's overall strategic planning process.

The ICAAP should produce a documented assessment that is reviewed and approved by the board at least annually. This documentation must include a description of the bank's risk appetite, the methodologies used for risk identification and quantification, the results of stress testing, and the capital plan. The ICAAP is subject to supervisory review as part of the SREP, and banks must be prepared to demonstrate the soundness and reliability of their internal assessments to their supervisors.

## Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP)

The Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP) represents the supervisory side of the Pillar 2 framework. Through the SREP, supervisory authorities conduct a comprehensive evaluation of individual banks' risk profiles, risk governance, capital adequacy, and liquidity positions. The SREP is designed to ensure that banks have adequate arrangements, strategies, processes, and mechanisms to maintain sufficient capital and liquidity to cover their risks.

Supervisors assess the quality and comprehensiveness of the bank's ICAAP, including the appropriateness of the methodologies employed, the accuracy of risk quantification, and the adequacy of internal capital targets. The SREP evaluation also considers qualitative factors such as the effectiveness of the bank's governance framework, the competence of senior management, and the quality of internal controls and audit functions.

Based on the SREP assessment, supervisors may require banks to hold additional capital above Pillar 1 minimums, known as Pillar 2 capital requirements (P2R) or Pillar 2 capital guidance (P2G). Supervisors may also impose qualitative requirements, such as improvements to risk management processes, governance structures, or data quality. The SREP outcome is communicated to the bank and typically includes a timeline for addressing identified deficiencies.

The BCBS expects that the SREP is conducted on a regular basis, with the frequency and intensity proportionate to the systemic importance, size, and risk profile of the institution. Globally systemically important banks (G-SIBs) are typically subject to more frequent and intensive supervisory review than smaller institutions.

## Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book (IRRBB)

Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book (IRRBB) is one of the most significant risks addressed under Pillar 2. The BCBS issued updated standards on IRRBB in April 2016 (BCBS Standard d368), which require banks to measure and control their exposure to changes in interest rates that affect the economic value and earnings of their banking book positions.

IRRBB arises from mismatches in the repricing characteristics of a bank's assets and liabilities. The three main sub-types of IRRBB are gap risk (arising from the timing of instrument rate changes), basis risk (arising from imperfect correlation in the adjustment of rates on different instruments with otherwise similar repricing characteristics), and option risk (arising from options embedded in banking book instruments, such as prepayment options on loans).

Banks are required to measure IRRBB using both economic value of equity (EVE) and net interest income (NII) approaches under a range of prescribed interest rate shock scenarios. The BCBS has defined six standardized shock scenarios for EVE and two for NII. Banks whose IRRBB exposure exceeds supervisory thresholds, defined as a decline in EVE greater than 15 percent of Tier 1 capital, are identified as outlier banks and may be subject to additional supervisory scrutiny and potential capital add-ons.

Effective IRRBB management requires robust governance frameworks, clearly defined risk appetite limits, comprehensive measurement systems, and regular reporting to senior management and the board. Banks must also incorporate IRRBB into their ICAAP and stress testing programs.

## Credit Concentration Risk

Credit concentration risk refers to the risk arising from an uneven distribution of exposures to individual counterparties, groups of connected counterparties, industries, economic sectors, or geographic regions. The BCBS has identified credit concentration risk as a material risk that is not fully captured by Pillar 1 capital requirements, making it a primary focus of Pillar 2 assessment.

There are two main dimensions of concentration risk: single-name concentration, which arises from large exposures to individual counterparties or groups of connected counterparties; and sectoral concentration, which arises from imperfect diversification across industries, geographies, or asset classes. Both dimensions can amplify losses during economic downturns and contribute to systemic risk when concentrations are correlated across institutions.

Under Pillar 2, banks are expected to have policies and processes for identifying, measuring, monitoring, and controlling concentration risk. This includes setting internal limits on large exposures, conducting regular portfolio concentration analyses, and incorporating concentration risk into the ICAAP. Supervisors evaluate the adequacy of these measures as part of the SREP and may impose additional capital requirements where concentrations are deemed excessive relative to the bank's capital base and risk management capacity.

## Liquidity Risk Management

Although the Basel III framework introduced dedicated quantitative liquidity standards under Pillar 1, including the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) and the Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR), Pillar 2 retains an important role in the supervisory assessment of liquidity risk management. The BCBS "Principles for Sound Liquidity Risk Management and Supervision" (BCBS144, September 2008) establishes comprehensive guidance that supervisors evaluate under the SREP.

Banks are expected to maintain a robust liquidity risk management framework that includes clearly articulated liquidity risk tolerance, appropriate governance and oversight structures, comprehensive measurement and monitoring systems, and well-developed contingency funding plans. The framework should cover all material sources of liquidity risk, including intraday liquidity risk, contingent liquidity risk from off-balance-sheet commitments, and cross-currency and cross-border liquidity risk.

Supervisors assess whether banks' liquidity risk management practices are commensurate with their size, complexity, and risk profile. Where deficiencies are identified, supervisors may require banks to enhance their liquidity buffers, improve their funding profiles, or strengthen their contingency planning. The integration of liquidity risk assessment into the broader SREP framework ensures that capital adequacy and liquidity adequacy are considered holistically.

## Stress Testing Requirements

Stress testing is a fundamental component of the Pillar 2 framework and a critical tool within the ICAAP. The BCBS guidance on stress testing, including the "Stress Testing Principles" issued in October 2018 (BCBS239 update), establishes expectations for banks and supervisors regarding the design, implementation, and use of stress tests.

Banks are required to conduct regular stress tests that assess the impact of severe but plausible adverse scenarios on their capital adequacy, profitability, and liquidity. Stress testing programs should cover all material risk types, including credit risk, market risk, operational risk, interest rate risk, and liquidity risk. Scenarios should be forward-looking and should incorporate both institution-specific and system-wide stress events.

The results of stress testing should directly inform capital planning and risk management decisions. Banks should integrate stress testing outcomes into the ICAAP, ensuring that internal capital targets are sufficient to absorb losses under stressed conditions without breaching minimum regulatory requirements. Senior management and the board should regularly review stress test results and take action to address identified vulnerabilities.

Supervisors use bank-level and system-wide stress testing as an input into the SREP. Supervisory stress tests may be conducted independently of banks' internal exercises and can serve as a basis for imposing additional capital requirements or buffers under Pillar 2.

## Corporate Governance and Risk Management

Pillar 2 places significant emphasis on the quality of corporate governance and risk management as determinants of a bank's overall safety and soundness. The BCBS "Corporate Governance Principles for Banks" (revised in July 2015) and the "Principles for the Sound Management of Operational Risk" provide detailed expectations that supervisors evaluate under the SREP.

Banks are expected to have governance structures that provide effective oversight of risk-taking activities. This includes a board of directors with appropriate expertise and independence, a clear organizational structure with well-defined lines of responsibility, effective internal control and audit functions, and a risk management function that is independent from business lines. The Chief Risk Officer (CRO) should have sufficient authority, stature, and resources to fulfill the risk management mandate.

The risk management framework should encompass a comprehensive risk appetite statement approved by the board, policies and procedures for all material risk types, robust risk measurement and reporting systems, and effective escalation mechanisms for limit breaches and emerging risks. The framework must be subject to regular independent review to ensure its continued effectiveness and relevance.

Supervisors assess governance and risk management quality as part of the SREP and may impose qualitative requirements, such as changes to governance structures, enhancements to risk management processes, or restrictions on certain business activities, where deficiencies are identified. Poor governance and weak risk management may also serve as a basis for requiring higher Pillar 2 capital buffers.

## Pillar 2 Capital Add-Ons

Pillar 2 capital add-ons represent additional capital that supervisors may require banks to hold above the Pillar 1 minimum. These add-ons are determined through the SREP process and are tailored to each institution's specific risk profile and circumstances. The BCBS framework distinguishes between Pillar 2 Requirements (P2R) and Pillar 2 Guidance (P2G).

Pillar 2 Requirements (P2R) are binding capital requirements that address risks not covered or not fully captured under Pillar 1. Common drivers of P2R include credit concentration risk, IRRBB, settlement risk, securitization risk not captured in Pillar 1, and model risk arising from the use of internal models. P2R are legally binding and a breach triggers mandatory supervisory intervention.

Pillar 2 Guidance (P2G) represents a supervisory expectation of the capital level that a bank should maintain above its binding capital requirements. P2G is typically informed by the results of supervisory stress tests and is intended to ensure that banks can absorb losses under adverse conditions without breaching their combined buffer requirement or P2R. While P2G is not legally binding in the same manner as P2R, a failure to meet P2G triggers a supervisory dialogue and may lead to more intensive monitoring.

The calibration of Pillar 2 capital add-ons requires supervisory judgment and must be based on a transparent methodology. Supervisors should communicate the rationale for add-ons clearly to the bank and provide an opportunity for the institution to present its own views before final determinations are made.

## Supervisory Intervention and Corrective Actions

The Pillar 2 framework provides supervisors with a range of tools for intervention when banks are found to have inadequate capital, deficient risk management, or other material weaknesses. The principle of early intervention is central to the framework, reflecting the BCBS view that prompt action reduces the likelihood of bank failures and minimizes their cost to the financial system and to depositors.

Supervisory responses are typically graduated and proportionate to the severity of the identified deficiencies. Initial measures may include enhanced monitoring, increased reporting requirements, or the issuance of supervisory recommendations for improvement. Where deficiencies are more significant, supervisors may impose formal requirements, such as mandatory capital restoration plans, restrictions on dividend payments and share buybacks, limitations on business expansion, or requirements to divest particular risk exposures.

In cases of severe deterioration, supervisors have the authority to require immediate capital increases, to impose restrictions on management activities, or to initiate resolution proceedings. The BCBS framework emphasizes that supervisors should have clear legal authority to take these actions and should be willing to exercise that authority in a timely manner.

Cross-border supervisory cooperation is a critical aspect of Pillar 2 implementation for internationally active banks. Home and host supervisors should maintain effective channels of communication, share relevant information on the risk profiles of banking groups, and coordinate their supervisory responses to ensure consistency and avoid gaps in oversight. Supervisory colleges, as promoted by the BCBS and the Financial Stability Board (FSB), serve as the primary mechanism for facilitating this coordination.

title: "Basel III - Pillar 2: Supervisory Review Process"
source: "Bank for International Settlements"
year: 2023
type: regulation
jurisdiction: international
regulatory_body: "Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)"
document_id: "BaselIII_Pillar2"
