    10→
    11→# LGPD - Brazilian General Data Protection Law (Lei 13.709/2018)
    12→
    13→The Brazilian General Data Protection Law (LGPD), enacted on August 14, 2018, establishes comprehensive rules for the collection, processing, storage, and sharing of personal data in Brazil. It applies to any natural person or legal entity, whether public or private, that processes personal data within Brazilian territory, when the processing activity aims to offer goods or services to individuals located in Brazil, or when the data was collected in Brazil. The LGPD is enforced by the National Data Protection Authority (ANPD).
    14→
    15→---
    16→
    17→## Chapter I - General Provisions (Articles 1-6)
    18→
    19→### Article 1 - Scope
    20→
    21→This law governs the processing of personal data, including by digital means, by any natural person or legal entity, whether public or private, with the purpose of protecting the fundamental rights of freedom, privacy, and the free development of the personality of the natural person. The law applies to data processing carried out in Brazilian territory, regardless of where the processor is headquartered.
    22→
    23→### Article 2 - Foundations
    24→
    25→The protection of personal data is grounded in the following foundations: respect for privacy; informational self-determination; freedom of expression, information, communication, and opinion; inviolability of intimacy, honor, and image; economic and technological development and innovation; free enterprise, free competition, and consumer protection; and human rights, free personality development, dignity, and the exercise of citizenship.
    26→
    27→### Article 3 - Territorial Application
    28→
    29→The LGPD applies to any processing operation carried out in Brazilian territory, regardless of the country where the data controller or processor is headquartered or where the data is located. The law also applies when the processing activity aims to offer or supply goods or services to data subjects located in Brazil, or when the personal data was collected in Brazilian territory.
    30→
    31→### Article 4 - Exclusions
    32→
    33→The LGPD does not apply to data processing carried out by a natural person exclusively for private and non-economic purposes. It also excludes processing performed exclusively for journalistic, artistic, academic, public safety, national defense, state security, or criminal investigation purposes. Processing of data originating from outside Brazil that is not shared with Brazilian processing agents is also excluded.
    34→
    35→### Article 5 - Definitions
    36→
    37→This article defines key terms used throughout the law. "Personal data" means any information related to an identified or identifiable natural person. "Sensitive personal data" includes data on racial or ethnic origin, religious conviction, political opinion, union membership, health, sex life, genetics, or biometrics. Other defined terms include "data subject," "controller," "processor," "data protection officer," "anonymization," "consent," and "blocking."
    38→
    39→### Article 6 - Principles
    40→
    41→Personal data processing activities must observe the following principles: purpose (processing for legitimate, specific, and informed purposes); adequacy (compatibility with the stated purposes); necessity (limitation to the minimum required); free access; data quality; transparency; security; prevention; non-discrimination; and accountability and demonstration. Controllers must be able to demonstrate compliance with these principles.
    42→
    43→---
    44→
    45→## Chapter II - Personal Data Processing (Articles 7-16)
    46→
    47→### Section I - Requirements for Processing (Articles 7-10)
    48→
    49→### Article 7 - Legal Bases for Processing
    50→
    51→Personal data may only be processed under specific legal bases: with the data subject's consent; for compliance with a legal or regulatory obligation; by public administration for public policy purposes; for studies by research entities using anonymization when possible; for the execution of a contract at the data subject's request; for the regular exercise of rights in judicial, administrative, or arbitration proceedings; for protection of life or physical safety; for health protection; for the legitimate interests of the controller or third party; or for credit protection.
    52→
    53→### Article 8 - Consent Requirements
    54→
    55→Consent must be provided in writing or by other means that demonstrate the data subject's will. When consent is given in writing, it must be in a separate clause, clearly distinguishable from other contractual matters. The burden of proving that consent was obtained in compliance with the law falls on the controller. Consent may be revoked at any time through an express statement by the data subject, via a free and facilitated procedure.
    56→
    57→### Article 9 - Right to Information
    58→
    59→The data subject has the right to facilitated access to information about the processing of their data. The controller must inform the subject about the specific purpose of the processing, the form and duration of the treatment, the identity of the controller, contact information, whether data is shared and for what purpose, and the responsibilities of the agents involved. When processing is based on consent, if the information provided is misleading, ambiguous, or abusive, the consent is considered void.
    60→
    61→### Article 10 - Legitimate Interest
    62→
    63→The legitimate interest of the controller may serve as a legal basis for processing personal data only for legitimate purposes, considered from concrete situations. These include support and promotion of the controller's activities and the protection of the data subject or third party in the regular exercise of their rights. Only strictly necessary data for the intended purpose may be processed under legitimate interest. The controller must adopt transparency measures and allow the data subject to exercise their rights.
    64→
    65→### Section II - Processing of Sensitive Personal Data (Articles 11-13)
    66→
    67→### Article 11 - Conditions for Processing Sensitive Data
    68→
    69→The processing of sensitive personal data may only occur when the data subject or legal guardian gives specific and highlighted consent for specific purposes, or without consent when indispensable for: compliance with a legal obligation; shared processing of data necessary for public policy execution; research by study entities ensuring anonymization; the regular exercise of rights including in contracts and judicial proceedings; protection of life or physical safety; health care in procedures performed by health professionals or entities; or fraud prevention and data subject safety.
    70→
    71→### Article 12 - Anonymized Data
    72→
    73→Anonymized data is not considered personal data under this law, unless the anonymization process can be reversed using reasonable technical means. The determination of what constitutes "reasonable means" must consider objective factors such as cost, time, available technology, and exclusive use of the means. Anonymized data may be considered personal data if used for behavioral profiling of a specific natural person, provided the person is identified.
    74→
    75→### Article 13 - Research and Public Health
    76→
    77→Studies in public health may use personal data processed exclusively within the research body and strictly for the purpose of carrying out studies and research. Data used for health studies must be kept in a controlled and secure environment, and whenever possible, anonymized or pseudonymized. The disclosure of results must not reveal individual personal data under any circumstances. The research body is responsible for information security.
    78→
    79→### Section III - Processing of Children's and Adolescents' Data (Article 14)
    80→
    81→### Article 14 - Children's and Adolescents' Data
    82→
    83→The processing of personal data of children and adolescents must be carried out in their best interest. Processing of children's data requires specific and highlighted consent from at least one parent or legal guardian. Controllers must make reasonable efforts to verify that consent was given by the parent or guardian, considering available technologies. Data collection of children may occur without parental consent only when necessary to contact parents or legal guardians, or for their protection. Controllers must not condition children's participation in games, internet applications, or other activities on the provision of personal data beyond what is strictly necessary for the activity.
    84→
    85→### Section IV - Completion of Processing (Articles 15-16)
    86→
    87→### Article 15 - Termination of Processing
    88→
    89→The processing of personal data ends when: the purpose has been achieved or the data is no longer necessary; the processing period has ended; the data subject communicates revocation of consent; or the national authority determines termination when there is a violation of the law's provisions. The controller must ensure that the termination of processing is carried out in compliance with the data subject's rights.
    90→
    91→### Article 16 - Deletion of Data
    92→
    93→Personal data must be deleted after the processing ends, with exceptions for: compliance with a legal or regulatory obligation by the controller; studies by research entities, ensuring anonymization when possible; transfer to third parties provided the legal requirements are met; or exclusive use by the controller, provided the data is anonymized and public access is not possible.
    94→
    95→---
    96→
    97→## Chapter III - Rights of the Data Subject (Articles 17-22)
    98→
    99→### Article 17 - Ownership of Personal Data
   100→
   101→Every natural person is guaranteed ownership of their personal data and is assured the fundamental rights of freedom, intimacy, and privacy under the terms of this law. The data subject holds sovereignty over their personal data and the right to exercise control over how it is processed.
   102→
   103→### Article 18 - Data Subject Rights
   104→
   105→The data subject has the right to obtain from the controller at any time, upon request: confirmation of the existence of processing; access to the data; correction of incomplete, inaccurate, or outdated data; anonymization, blocking, or deletion of unnecessary or excessive data; portability of data to another service or product provider; deletion of personal data processed with consent; information about public and private entities with which the controller has shared data; information about the possibility of denying consent and its consequences; and revocation of consent.
   106→
   107→### Article 19 - Confirmation and Access
   108→
   109→Confirmation of the existence of processing and access to data must be provided within 15 days from the date of the data subject's request, in a clear, adequate format, or through a detailed statement within a reasonable time. The data may be provided electronically, securely, or in printed form, at the data subject's choice. When processing originates from consent or contract, the data subject may request a complete electronic copy of their personal data.
   110→
   111→### Article 20 - Automated Decision Review
   112→
   113→The data subject has the right to request a review of decisions made solely on the basis of automated processing of personal data that affect their interests, including decisions that define their personal, professional, consumer, or credit profile, or aspects of their personality. The controller must provide clear and adequate information about the criteria and procedures used for automated decision-making, subject to trade and industrial secrets.
   114→
   115→### Article 21 - Defense of Interests
   116→
   117→Personal data related to the regular exercise of rights by the data subject may not be used against them. The use of personal data cannot cause harm to the data subject through discriminatory or abusive practices.
   118→
   119→### Article 22 - Protection in Judicial Proceedings
   120→
   121→The defense of data subject interests may be exercised individually or collectively in court, as provided by applicable legislation on procedural instruments for individual, collective, and diffuse protection. The data subject may petition the ANPD for the protection of their personal data rights.
   122→
   123→---
   124→
   125→## Chapter IV - Processing of Personal Data by the Government (Articles 23-30)
   126→
   127→### Article 23 - Public Administration Processing
   128→
   129→The processing of personal data by public-law entities must be carried out for the performance of their legal duties, for the execution of public policies, and must be informed to the data subject through clear and updated information about the legal basis, purpose, and procedures used. Public entities must provide information about the legal basis, purpose, and applicable procedures in easily accessible formats.
   130→
   131→### Article 24 - State-Owned Enterprises
   132→
   133→State-owned enterprises and mixed-capital companies that operate in a competitive, market regime are subject to the same rules applicable to private entities. When these entities act in the implementation of public policies, they receive the same treatment as public-law entities under this chapter.
   134→
   135→### Article 25 - Data in Interoperable Format
   136→
   137→Public data must be kept in an interoperable, structured format for shared use, with a view to the execution of public policies, the provision of public services, decentralization of public activity, and the dissemination and access to information by the public. The requirements of data security and protection must be observed in shared use.
   138→
   139→### Article 26 - Shared Use by Government
   140→
   141→The shared use of personal data by the government must meet specific public purposes, in compliance with the law, and must pursue objectives such as the execution of public policies. Shared use of data for purposes incompatible with those for which it was originally collected is prohibited. The sharing of data between government entities must be communicated to the ANPD. Transfer of personal data to private entities is forbidden, except in cases of decentralized execution of public activity, when data is publicly accessible, or when there is a specific legal provision.
   142→
   143→### Article 27 - External Communication of Government Data
   144→
   145→The communication or shared use of personal data held by public-law entities with private-law entities depends on the consent of the data subject, unless specific exceptions apply. These exceptions include cases where the sharing is necessary to prevent fraud, or when the data is already publicly accessible.
   146→
   147→### Article 28 - Notary and Registry Bodies
   148→
   149→Notary and registry bodies that act by public delegation are subject to the provisions of this law when processing personal data. They must ensure the transparency and protection of personal data in accordance with the standards established by the national authority.
   150→
   151→### Article 29 - ANPD Oversight of Government
   152→
   153→The ANPD may request information at any time from public entities that carry out processing operations of personal data. It may issue opinions and recommendations to ensure compliance with this law by the public sector and may suggest the adoption of standards and good practices for the processing of personal data by the government.
   154→
   155→### Article 30 - ANPD Report on Government Processing
   156→
   157→The ANPD may determine that a public entity publish impact assessment reports on personal data protection, as well as suggest the adoption of administrative measures to ensure the cessation of violations found. Government entities must cooperate with ANPD requests and provide all information necessary for assessments.
   158→
   159→---
   160→
   161→## Chapter V - International Transfer of Data (Articles 33-36)
   162→
   163→### Article 33 - Conditions for International Transfer
   164→
   165→International transfer of personal data is only permitted in specific situations: to countries or international organizations that provide an adequate degree of data protection; when the controller offers and proves guarantees of compliance through specific contractual clauses, standard contractual clauses, global corporate standards, or regularly issued seals and certificates; when the transfer is necessary for international legal cooperation; when authorized by the ANPD; when resulting from an international cooperation agreement; for the protection of life or physical safety; or when the ANPD authorizes it.
   166→
   167→### Article 34 - Adequate Level of Protection
   168→
   169→The adequate level of protection of a country or international organization is assessed by the ANPD, taking into account the general and sectoral legislation in force in the country of destination; the nature of the data; compliance with general data protection principles; the adoption of security measures; the existence of judicial and institutional guarantees for the respect of data protection rights; and other specific circumstances related to the transfer.
   170→
   171→### Article 35 - Cooperation Agreements
   172→
   173→The definition of content for standard contractual clauses, verification of the adequacy guarantees offered by global corporate standards, and determination of standard clauses for international data transfer shall be carried out by the ANPD. International cooperation agreements may serve as a legal basis for international data transfer.
   174→
   175→### Article 36 - Changes in Adequacy Level
   176→
   177→Changes in the level of protection offered by a country or international organization may be communicated by the ANPD to the controllers, so that they may adopt necessary measures to ensure compliance with the principles and guarantees established in this law. The ANPD may revoke adequacy decisions at any time.
   178→
   179→---
   180→
   181→## Chapter VI - Data Processing Agents (Articles 37-45)
   182→
   183→### Section I - Controller and Processor (Articles 37-40)
   184→
   185→### Article 37 - Records of Processing Activities
   186→
   187→The controller and processor must maintain records of the personal data processing operations they carry out, especially when the processing is based on legitimate interest. The records must contain at least the types of personal data collected, the legal basis for processing, the purposes, and data retention periods.
   188→
   189→### Article 38 - Impact Assessment Report
   190→
   191→The ANPD may determine that the controller prepare a Data Protection Impact Assessment Report (RIPD), including sensitive data, with a description of the types of data collected, the methodology used for collection and data security, and the risk analysis and mitigation measures adopted. The report must contain a description of the processing operations that may generate risks to civil liberties and fundamental rights.
   192→
   193→### Article 39 - Processor Obligations
   194→
   195→The processor must carry out the processing of personal data according to the instructions provided by the controller, who shall verify compliance with its own instructions and the applicable regulations. The processor may not process personal data in a manner that exceeds the controller's instructions unless required by law.
   196→
   197→### Article 40 - ANPD Standards for Processing Agents
   198→
   199→The ANPD may establish standards for technical reports and complementary norms for processing activities that entail high risk to the guarantee of the principles established in this law. The ANPD may also provide simplified standards for small and medium-sized enterprises, startups, and similar entities.
   200→
   201→### Section II - Data Protection Officer (Article 41)
   202→
   203→### Article 41 - Data Protection Officer (DPO)
   204→
   205→The controller must appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO), whose identity and contact information must be publicly disclosed, preferably on the controller's website. The DPO's activities include: accepting complaints and communications from data subjects, providing clarifications and taking measures; receiving communications from the ANPD and taking measures; guiding the entity's employees and contractors regarding data protection practices; and performing other duties determined by the controller or established in complementary regulations. The ANPD may establish complementary norms on the definition and duties of the DPO, including cases where the appointment may be waived based on the nature and size of the entity or the volume of data processing operations.
   206→
   207→### Section III - Liability and Indemnification (Articles 42-45)
   208→
   209→### Article 42 - Compensation for Damages
   210→
   211→The controller or processor that causes material, moral, individual, or collective damage to others through the exercise of personal data processing activity, in violation of data protection legislation, is obliged to repair the damage. The processor is jointly liable when it fails to comply with the obligations of the data protection legislation or when it does not follow the lawful instructions of the controller. The courts may, in civil proceedings, invert the burden of proof in favor of the data subject when the allegation is plausible or when the production of evidence is excessively burdensome for the data subject.
   212→
   213→### Article 43 - Exclusions from Liability
   214→
   215→Processing agents are not held liable when they prove that they did not carry out the processing of personal data attributed to them; that, although they carried out the processing, there was no violation of data protection legislation; or that the damage is due to the exclusive fault of the data subject or a third party. The burden of proving the exclusion of liability falls on the processing agents.
   216→
   217→### Article 44 - Irregular Processing
   218→
   219→The processing of personal data is considered irregular when it does not comply with the legislation or when it does not provide the security that the data subject can reasonably expect, considering the circumstances. The processor is liable for damages caused by the processing of personal data when it fails to adopt the security measures provided for in this law or in other applicable regulations.
   220→
   221→### Article 45 - Consumer Protection Application
   222→
   223→The hypotheses of civil liability violation in the relations of consumption remain subject to the rules provided in the relevant legislation, including the Consumer Defense Code. Data protection violations in consumer relations are also subject to the sanctions and procedures established in consumer protection legislation.
   224→
   225→---
   226→
   227→## Chapter VII - Security and Good Practices (Articles 46-51)
   228→
   229→### Section I - Data Security (Articles 46-49)
   230→
   231→### Article 46 - Security Measures
   232→
   233→Processing agents must adopt security, technical, and administrative measures capable of protecting personal data from unauthorized access and accidental or unlawful situations of destruction, loss, alteration, communication, or any form of improper or unlawful processing. The ANPD may establish minimum technical standards for data protection, considering the nature of the data processed, the specific characteristics of the processing, and the current state of technology.
   234→
   235→### Article 47 - Security by Design
   236→
   237→Processing agents or any person who intervenes in one of the processing phases are obligated to guarantee the security of the information throughout the entire processing lifecycle, even after its conclusion. Security measures must be applied from the design phase of the product or service through its entire lifecycle.
   238→
   239→### Article 48 - Data Breach Notification
   240→
   241→The controller must notify the ANPD and the data subject of the occurrence of a security incident that may create a risk or relevant damage to the data subjects within a reasonable time, as defined by the ANPD. The notification must mention at minimum: the description of the nature of the affected personal data; information about the data subjects involved; the technical and security measures used for data protection; the risks related to the incident; the reasons for any delay in communication; and the measures adopted to reverse or mitigate the effects of the incident.
   242→
   243→### Article 49 - Information Systems Design
   244→
   245→Information systems used for the processing of personal data must be structured to meet security requirements, good practice standards, and governance principles, as well as the general principles established in this law and other applicable regulations. Systems must be designed with privacy by default and by design.
   246→
   247→### Section II - Good Practices and Governance (Articles 50-51)
   248→
   249→### Article 50 - Good Practice Programs
   250→
   251→Controllers and processors may, individually or through associations, formulate rules of good practice and governance that establish the conditions of organization, operating procedures, complaint and petition mechanisms, applicable norms, educational actions, internal oversight mechanisms, risk mitigation mechanisms, and other aspects related to the processing of personal data. Good practice programs must include a minimum set of requirements: demonstration of the controller's commitment; applicability to the entire set of personal data under its control; adaptation to the structure, scale, and volume of operations; establishment of policies and safeguards based on a risk assessment process; and the creation of a relationship of trust with the data subject.
   252→
   253→### Article 51 - ANPD Encouragement of Standards
   254→
   255→The ANPD shall encourage the adoption of technical standards that facilitate control by data subjects over their personal data. The authority shall promote good practices and governance standards, considering the specificities of the processing operations and the nature of the data processed.
   256→
   257→---
   258→
   259→## Chapter VIII - Enforcement (Articles 52-54)
   260→
   261→### Article 52 - Administrative Sanctions
   262→
   263→Processing agents that commit infractions against the provisions of this law are subject to the following administrative sanctions, applied by the ANPD: a warning, with an indication of a deadline for adopting corrective measures; a simple fine of up to 2% of the revenue of the private legal entity, group, or conglomerate in Brazil in the most recent fiscal year, excluding taxes, limited to a total of R$ 50 million per infraction; a daily fine, subject to the same total limit; publication of the infraction after it has been duly investigated and confirmed; blocking of the personal data to which the infraction refers until its regularization; deletion of the personal data to which the infraction refers; partial suspension of the database operation for a maximum period of six months, renewable for an equal period; suspension of the processing activity for a maximum period of six months, renewable for an equal period; or partial or total prohibition of activities related to data processing.
   264→
   265→### Article 53 - Sanctioning Regulations
   266→
   267→The ANPD shall define, through regulation, the sanctioning methodologies to be adopted by the authority, which must be the subject of prior public consultation. The regulation must establish the specific circumstances and conditions for the application of sanctions, the forms and dosimetries for calculating the base value of fines, and the cases in which penalties may be mitigated or aggravated.
   268→
   269→### Article 54 - Proportionality and Adequacy of Sanctions
   270→
   271→The sanctions established in this law must be applied after an administrative procedure that provides for an opportunity for full defense, in a gradual, isolated, or cumulative manner, according to the peculiarities of the specific case. The ANPD must consider the gravity and nature of the infractions, the good faith of the infractor, the economic condition of the infractor, recidivism, the degree of damage caused, the adoption of internal mechanisms and procedures for data protection, the adoption of a data protection policy, and prompt adoption of corrective measures.
   272→
   273→---
   274→
   275→## Chapter IX - National Data Protection Authority (ANPD) (Articles 55-A to 55-L)
   276→
   277→### Article 55-A - Establishment of the ANPD
   278→
   279→The National Data Protection Authority (ANPD) is established as a body of the federal public administration, part of the Presidency of the Republic. The ANPD has technical and decision-making autonomy and its own assets. The ANPD's nature is transitory and may be transformed into an independent entity linked to the Presidency of the Republic after two years.
   280→
   281→### Article 55-B - Legal Nature
   282→
   283→The ANPD has technical and decision-making autonomy. The legal nature of the ANPD is established as a special autarchic regime, guaranteeing greater independence and autonomy in the exercise of its competencies.
   284→
   285→### Article 55-C - Composition
   286→
   287→The ANPD is composed of: the Board of Directors, the highest deliberative body; the National Council for the Protection of Personal Data and Privacy; the Internal Affairs Division; an Ombudsman; its own legal advisory body; and administrative and specialized units required for the application of this law.
   288→
   289→### Article 55-D - Board of Directors
   290→
   291→The Board of Directors of the ANPD is composed of five directors, including the Director-President. The members of the Board of Directors are appointed by the President of the Republic, after approval by the Federal Senate, and serve a four-year term. The members are chosen from among Brazilian citizens with an unblemished reputation, a university degree, and a distinguished professional record in the field of personal data protection, information and communication technologies, or related areas.
   292→
   293→### Article 55-E - Dismissal of Directors
   294→
   295→Members of the ANPD Board of Directors may only be removed from office in cases of resignation, final judicial conviction, or administrative disciplinary proceedings. The dismissal of a director before the end of their term may only occur upon a reasoned request from the President of the Republic, after approval by an absolute majority of the Federal Senate.
   296→
   297→### Article 55-F - Quarantine Period
   298→
   299→Directors of the ANPD are subject to a quarantine period of six months after leaving office, during which they are barred from exercising any activity in the private sector related to data protection. During the quarantine period, former directors receive the same compensation as they did in office.
   300→
   301→### Article 55-G - Conflict of Interest
   302→
   303→Directors of the ANPD are subject to the provisions of the Conflict of Interest Law (Law 12.813/2013). The ethical and disciplinary standards applicable to federal public servants apply to all ANPD personnel.
   304→
   305→### Article 55-H - Revenue and Budget
   306→
   307→The ANPD's revenue includes budgetary allocations; donations; amounts collected from the application of sanctions; and amounts derived from agreements, contracts, or covenants it enters into. The ANPD shall collect revenue from the amounts arising from the application of fines for violations of this law.
   308→
   309→### Article 55-I - ANPD Personnel
   310→
   311→The ANPD personnel are governed by the federal civil servants' regime. The ANPD may requisition federal public servants for temporary assignment.
   312→
   313→### Article 55-J - ANPD Competencies
   314→
   315→The ANPD is responsible for: ensuring the protection of personal data in accordance with this legislation; preparing guidelines for a National Policy on the Protection of Personal Data and Privacy; overseeing and applying sanctions in cases of data processing done in violation of the legislation; promoting awareness and understanding of data protection norms and policies; stimulating the adoption of standards for services and products that facilitate data subjects' control over their data; issuing opinions and administrative procedures on data protection; promoting cooperation actions with data protection authorities of other countries; and editing regulations and procedures on personal data protection and privacy.
   316→
   317→### Article 55-K - National Council Composition
   318→
   319→The National Council for the Protection of Personal Data and Privacy is composed of 23 representatives, appointed by the President of the Republic, from government, civil society, scientific institutions, and the private sector. The Council's role is advisory and its members serve two-year terms.
   320→
   321→### Article 55-L - National Council Competencies
   322→
   323→The National Council for the Protection of Personal Data and Privacy has the following competencies: proposing strategic guidelines and providing input on the National Policy for Personal Data Protection and Privacy; preparing annual reports assessing the implementation of this law; suggesting actions to be taken by the ANPD; carrying out studies, debates, and public hearings on the protection of personal data; and disseminating knowledge about personal data protection and privacy to the public.
   324→
   325→---
   326→
   327→## Chapter X - Final and Transitional Provisions (Articles 56-65)
   328→
   329→### Article 56 - Transitional Provision for ANPD
   330→
   331→The ANPD and the National Council for the Protection of Personal Data and Privacy must be created within 28 days from the publication of the law that created the ANPD. The provisions of this chapter apply until the ANPD is fully structured.
   332→
   333→### Article 57 - ANPD Transition Costs
   334→
   335→The transitional costs for the implementation of the ANPD shall be borne by the budget of the Presidency of the Republic. The funding necessary for the ANPD's initial operations must be provided through specific budgetary provisions.
   336→
   337→### Article 58 - Former Provision (Revoked)
   338→
   339→This article was revoked by Provisional Measure 869/2018, which restructured the composition and governance model of the ANPD. Its provisions have been superseded by the articles establishing the current ANPD structure.
   340→
   341→### Article 59 - Former Provision (Revoked)
   342→
   343→This article was also revoked by Provisional Measure 869/2018 as part of the reorganization of the ANPD's institutional framework. The provisions originally contained here have been replaced by the updated Chapter IX articles.
   344→
   345→### Article 60 - Amendments to Consumer Defense Code
   346→
   347→This article amends Article 7, item X, of the Consumer Defense Code (Law 8.078/1990) to include the protection of personal data in consumer relations. It explicitly states that the protection and treatment of personal data is a consumer right, linking the consumer protection and data protection regulatory frameworks.
   348→
   349→### Article 61 - Amendments to Civil Registry Law
   350→
   351→This article amends the Public Records Law to include requirements for the protection of personal data in public registry activities. Registrars must comply with the personal data protection provisions of this law when processing data in the exercise of their delegated public functions.
   352→
   353→### Article 62 - Amendments to Access to Information Law
   354→
   355→This article provides that the ANPD and the entities responsible for access to information oversight shall coordinate their activities to ensure the harmonious application of data protection and access to information legislation. The processing of personal data by public entities must comply with both transparency requirements and data protection obligations.
   356→
   357→### Article 63 - Existing Rights Preserved
   358→
   359→The rights and principles established in this law do not exclude others provided for in the legal system related to the protection of personal data, including those derived from international treaties and conventions to which Brazil is a party. The LGPD complements rather than replaces existing privacy and data protection norms.
   360→
   361→### Article 64 - Sectoral Rights Preserved
   362→
   363→The rights and principles expressed in this law do not exclude others provided for in Brazilian law, including the Consumer Defense Code, the Civil Rights Framework for the Internet (Marco Civil da Internet, Law 12.965/2014), and other sectoral legislation. This article ensures the complementary nature of the LGPD with other regulatory frameworks.
   364→
   365→### Article 65 - Entry into Force
   366→
   367→This law entered into force as follows: Articles 55-A through 55-L, 58-A, and 58-B entered into force on December 28, 2018 (the date of publication of the converting law); Articles 52, 53, and 54 (administrative sanctions) entered into force on August 1, 2021; and the remaining articles entered into force on September 18, 2020, after the vacatio legis period. The staggered entry into force allowed organizations time to adapt their processes and systems to the new data protection requirements.

# LGPD - Brazilian General Data Protection Law (Lei 13.709/2018)

The Brazilian General Data Protection Law (LGPD), enacted on August 14, 2018, establishes comprehensive rules for the collection, processing, storage, and sharing of personal data in Brazil. It applies to any natural person or legal entity that processes personal data within Brazilian territory or targeting individuals located in Brazil. The LGPD is enforced by the National Data Protection Authority (ANPD).

---

## Chapter I - General Provisions (Articles 1-6)

### Article 1 - Scope

This law governs the processing of personal data, including by digital means, by any natural person or legal entity, with the purpose of protecting fundamental rights of freedom, privacy, and the free development of personality. It applies to processing carried out in Brazilian territory regardless of the processor's headquarters.

### Article 2 - Foundations

Data protection is grounded in respect for privacy, informational self-determination, freedom of expression, inviolability of intimacy, economic and technological development, free enterprise, consumer protection, and human rights and dignity.

### Article 3 - Territorial Application

The LGPD applies to any processing operation carried out in Brazil, when the activity targets data subjects in Brazil, or when personal data was collected in Brazilian territory. The law applies regardless of the controller's or processor's country of headquarters.

### Article 4 - Exclusions

The LGPD does not apply to processing by natural persons for exclusively private non-economic purposes, nor to processing exclusively for journalistic, artistic, academic, public safety, national defense, state security, or criminal investigation purposes.

### Article 5 - Definitions

Key terms defined include: "personal data" (information related to an identified or identifiable natural person), "sensitive personal data" (racial/ethnic origin, religious conviction, political opinion, union membership, health, sex life, genetics, biometrics), "controller," "processor," "data protection officer," "anonymization," "consent," and "blocking."

### Article 6 - Principles

Processing must observe the principles of: purpose, adequacy, necessity, free access, data quality, transparency, security, prevention, non-discrimination, and accountability. Controllers must demonstrate compliance with these principles.

---

## Chapter II - Personal Data Processing (Articles 7-16)

### Section I - Requirements for Processing (Articles 7-10)

### Article 7 - Legal Bases for Processing

Personal data may only be processed under ten legal bases: consent, legal obligation, public policy execution, research with anonymization, contract execution, exercise of rights in proceedings, protection of life, health protection, legitimate interest, or credit protection.

### Article 8 - Consent Requirements

Consent must be provided in writing or other demonstrable means, as a separate clause distinct from other contractual matters. The controller bears the burden of proving valid consent, and consent may be revoked at any time via a free and facilitated procedure.

### Article 9 - Right to Information

Data subjects have the right to information about the purpose, duration, controller identity, data sharing practices, and agent responsibilities related to processing. Consent based on misleading, ambiguous, or abusive information is void.

### Article 10 - Legitimate Interest

Legitimate interest as a legal basis requires concrete, legitimate purposes such as supporting the controller's activities or protecting the data subject. Only strictly necessary data may be processed, and the controller must adopt transparency measures.

### Section II - Processing of Sensitive Personal Data (Articles 11-13)

### Article 11 - Conditions for Processing Sensitive Data

Sensitive data processing requires specific and highlighted consent, or may proceed without consent when indispensable for legal obligation compliance, public policy, research with anonymization, exercise of rights, protection of life, health care by professionals, or fraud prevention.

### Article 12 - Anonymized Data

Anonymized data is not considered personal data unless the anonymization can be reversed using reasonable technical means. Data used for behavioral profiling of an identifiable person may still be treated as personal data.

### Article 13 - Research and Public Health

Health research data must be processed exclusively within research bodies in controlled, secure environments, with anonymization or pseudonymization whenever possible. Results must never reveal individual personal data.

### Section III - Processing of Children's and Adolescents' Data (Article 14)

### Article 14 - Children's and Adolescents' Data

Processing of children's data requires specific consent from a parent or legal guardian and must serve the child's best interest. Controllers must not condition children's participation in activities on provision of data beyond what is strictly necessary.

### Section IV - Completion of Processing (Articles 15-16)

### Article 15 - Termination of Processing

Processing ends when the purpose is achieved, the period expires, the data subject revokes consent, or the ANPD determines termination due to a violation.

### Article 16 - Deletion of Data

Data must be deleted after processing ends, except for legal obligations, research with anonymization, lawful transfer to third parties, or exclusive controller use with anonymization.

---

## Chapter III - Rights of the Data Subject (Articles 17-22)

### Article 17 - Ownership of Personal Data

Every natural person is guaranteed ownership of their personal data and the fundamental rights of freedom, intimacy, and privacy.

### Article 18 - Data Subject Rights

Data subjects may request: confirmation of processing, data access, correction, anonymization/blocking/deletion of unnecessary data, data portability, deletion of consent-based data, information on shared data recipients, information on consequences of denying consent, and consent revocation.

### Article 19 - Confirmation and Access

Confirmation and data access must be provided within 15 days of the request, in clear format or detailed statement. Data subjects may request a complete electronic copy of their data when processing is based on consent or contract.

### Article 20 - Automated Decision Review

Data subjects may request review of decisions made solely through automated processing that affect their interests, including profiling decisions. Controllers must disclose criteria and procedures used, subject to trade secrets.

### Article 21 - Defense of Interests

Personal data related to the exercise of a data subject's rights may not be used against them. Data use must not cause harm through discriminatory or abusive practices.

### Article 22 - Protection in Judicial Proceedings

Data subject interests may be defended individually or collectively in court. Data subjects may also petition the ANPD for protection of their rights.

---

## Chapter IV - Processing of Personal Data by the Government (Articles 23-30)

### Article 23 - Public Administration Processing

Public entities must process personal data for the performance of legal duties and public policy execution, with clear and updated information provided to data subjects about the legal basis, purpose, and procedures.

### Article 24 - State-Owned Enterprises

State-owned enterprises operating in competitive markets follow private-entity rules. When implementing public policies, they follow public-entity rules.

### Article 25 - Data in Interoperable Format

Public data must be maintained in interoperable, structured formats for shared use in public policy execution and service provision, observing security and protection requirements.

### Article 26 - Shared Use by Government

Government data sharing must serve specific public purposes and may not pursue incompatible objectives. Transfer to private entities is generally forbidden except for decentralized public activities, publicly accessible data, or specific legal provisions.

### Article 27 - External Communication of Government Data

Sharing personal data held by public entities with private entities requires data subject consent, except for fraud prevention or when data is publicly accessible.

### Article 28 - Notary and Registry Bodies

Notary and registry bodies acting under public delegation must comply with this law and ensure data transparency and protection per ANPD standards.

### Article 29 - ANPD Oversight of Government

The ANPD may request information from public entities at any time, issue opinions and recommendations, and suggest adoption of standards and good practices for government data processing.

### Article 30 - ANPD Report on Government Processing

The ANPD may require public entities to publish data protection impact assessment reports and suggest administrative measures to address violations.

---

## Chapter V - International Transfer of Data (Articles 33-36)

### Article 33 - Conditions for International Transfer

International data transfer is permitted only to countries with adequate protection levels, when the controller demonstrates compliance guarantees through contractual clauses or certifications, for international legal cooperation, when authorized by ANPD, or for protection of life or physical safety.

### Article 34 - Adequate Level of Protection

The ANPD assesses adequacy based on the destination country's legislation, data nature, compliance with data protection principles, security measures, and judicial and institutional guarantees.

### Article 35 - Cooperation Agreements

The ANPD defines standard contractual clauses and verifies adequacy guarantees for global corporate standards. International cooperation agreements may serve as legal bases for transfer.

### Article 36 - Changes in Adequacy Level

The ANPD may communicate changes in a country's protection level to controllers and may revoke adequacy decisions at any time, requiring controllers to adopt compliance measures.

---

## Chapter VI - Data Processing Agents (Articles 37-45)

### Section I - Controller and Processor (Articles 37-40)

### Article 37 - Records of Processing Activities

Controllers and processors must maintain records of processing operations, including data types, legal bases, purposes, and retention periods, especially when processing is based on legitimate interest.

### Article 38 - Impact Assessment Report

The ANPD may require controllers to prepare a Data Protection Impact Assessment Report (RIPD) describing data types, collection methodology, security measures, and risk analysis with mitigation measures.

### Article 39 - Processor Obligations

Processors must process data strictly according to controller instructions and may not exceed those instructions unless required by law.

### Article 40 - ANPD Standards for Processing Agents

The ANPD may establish standards for high-risk processing activities and provide simplified standards for small and medium-sized enterprises and startups.

### Section II - Data Protection Officer (Article 41)

### Article 41 - Data Protection Officer (DPO)

Controllers must appoint a DPO whose identity must be publicly disclosed. DPO duties include handling data subject complaints, receiving ANPD communications, guiding employees on data protection, and other controller-defined duties. The ANPD may waive the appointment requirement based on entity size or data volume.

### Section III - Liability and Indemnification (Articles 42-45)

### Article 42 - Compensation for Damages

Controllers or processors that cause material or moral damage through data processing violations must repair the damage. Processors are jointly liable when they fail to comply with legal obligations or controller instructions. Courts may invert the burden of proof in favor of data subjects.

### Article 43 - Exclusions from Liability

Processing agents are not liable when they prove they did not perform the attributed processing, there was no violation, or the damage resulted exclusively from the data subject's or a third party's fault.

### Article 44 - Irregular Processing

Processing is irregular when it violates legislation or fails to provide reasonably expected security. Processors are liable when they fail to adopt required security measures.

### Article 45 - Consumer Protection Application

Civil liability for data protection violations in consumer relations remains subject to consumer protection legislation, including the Consumer Defense Code.

---

## Chapter VII - Security and Good Practices (Articles 46-51)

### Section I - Data Security (Articles 46-49)

### Article 46 - Security Measures

Processing agents must adopt technical and administrative security measures to protect data from unauthorized access, destruction, loss, alteration, or unlawful processing. The ANPD may establish minimum technical standards.

### Article 47 - Security by Design

All parties involved in processing must guarantee information security throughout the entire processing lifecycle, from the design phase through conclusion.

### Article 48 - Data Breach Notification

Controllers must notify the ANPD and affected data subjects of security incidents that may create risk or relevant damage within a reasonable time. Notifications must describe the affected data, subjects involved, security measures, risks, delay reasons, and mitigation measures.

### Article 49 - Information Systems Design

Systems used for personal data processing must be structured to meet security requirements, good practices, and governance principles, incorporating privacy by default and by design.

### Section II - Good Practices and Governance (Articles 50-51)

### Article 50 - Good Practice Programs

Controllers and processors may formulate governance rules covering organizational conditions, complaint mechanisms, educational actions, oversight, and risk mitigation. Programs must demonstrate controller commitment, cover all personal data, adapt to operational scale, and include risk-based safeguards.

### Article 51 - ANPD Encouragement of Standards

The ANPD shall encourage adoption of technical standards facilitating data subject control and promote good practices and governance standards appropriate to specific processing contexts.

---

## Chapter VIII - Enforcement (Articles 52-54)

### Article 52 - Administrative Sanctions

Sanctions include: warnings with corrective deadlines; fines up to 2% of revenue in Brazil (capped at R$ 50 million per infraction); daily fines; publication of the infraction; blocking or deletion of affected data; partial suspension of database operations (up to six months, renewable); suspension of processing activities (up to six months, renewable); or partial/total prohibition of data processing activities.

### Article 53 - Sanctioning Regulations

The ANPD shall define sanctioning methodologies through regulation after public consultation, establishing circumstances for sanction application, fine calculation methods, and aggravating or mitigating factors.

### Article 54 - Proportionality and Adequacy of Sanctions

Sanctions require an administrative procedure with full defense rights and must consider infraction gravity, good faith, economic condition, recidivism, degree of damage, adoption of internal data protection mechanisms, and prompt corrective measures.

---

## Chapter IX - National Data Protection Authority (ANPD) (Articles 55-A to 55-L)

### Article 55-A - Establishment of the ANPD

The ANPD is established as a federal public administration body with technical and decision-making autonomy. Its nature is transitory and may be transformed into an independent entity after two years.

### Article 55-B - Legal Nature

The ANPD operates under a special autarchic regime, guaranteeing independence and autonomy in exercising its competencies.

### Article 55-C - Composition

The ANPD comprises: the Board of Directors (highest deliberative body), the National Council for Personal Data Protection and Privacy, Internal Affairs Division, Ombudsman, legal advisory body, and specialized administrative units.

### Article 55-D - Board of Directors

The Board consists of five directors including the Director-President, appointed by the President of the Republic after Senate approval, serving four-year terms. Directors must have distinguished professional records in data protection or related fields.

### Article 55-E - Dismissal of Directors

Directors may only be removed for resignation, final judicial conviction, or disciplinary proceedings. Early dismissal requires a reasoned presidential request approved by an absolute Senate majority.

### Article 55-F - Quarantine Period

Directors face a six-month quarantine after leaving office, barring private-sector data protection activities. They receive full compensation during this period.

### Article 55-G - Conflict of Interest

ANPD directors are subject to the Conflict of Interest Law (Law 12.813/2013) and federal public servant ethical and disciplinary standards.

### Article 55-H - Revenue and Budget

ANPD revenue includes budgetary allocations, donations, sanction proceeds, and amounts from agreements and contracts.

### Article 55-I - ANPD Personnel

ANPD personnel follow the federal civil servants' regime, and the authority may requisition federal servants for temporary assignment.

### Article 55-J - ANPD Competencies

The ANPD ensures data protection compliance, prepares national policy guidelines, oversees and sanctions violations, promotes awareness, stimulates adoption of data control standards, issues opinions, promotes international cooperation, and edits regulations on data protection.

### Article 55-K - National Council Composition

The National Council comprises 23 representatives from government, civil society, scientific institutions, and the private sector, serving two-year advisory terms.

### Article 55-L - National Council Competencies

The Council proposes strategic guidelines, prepares annual implementation reports, suggests ANPD actions, conducts studies and public hearings, and disseminates data protection knowledge.

---

## Chapter X - Final and Transitional Provisions (Articles 56-65)

### Article 56 - Transitional Provision for ANPD

The ANPD and National Council must be created within 28 days of the establishing law's publication. Transitional provisions apply until the ANPD is fully structured.

### Article 57 - ANPD Transition Costs

Transitional implementation costs are borne by the Presidency of the Republic's budget.

### Article 58 - Former Provision (Revoked)

Revoked by Provisional Measure 869/2018, which restructured the ANPD's governance model. Superseded by current Chapter IX articles.

### Article 59 - Former Provision (Revoked)

Also revoked by Provisional Measure 869/2018 as part of the ANPD institutional reorganization. Replaced by updated Chapter IX provisions.

### Article 60 - Amendments to Consumer Defense Code

Amends the Consumer Defense Code (Law 8.078/1990) to include personal data protection as a consumer right, linking consumer protection and data protection frameworks.

### Article 61 - Amendments to Civil Registry Law

Amends the Public Records Law to require personal data protection compliance in public registry activities performed under delegated public functions.

### Article 62 - Amendments to Access to Information Law

Requires coordination between the ANPD and access-to-information oversight entities to harmonize data protection and transparency obligations for public entities.

### Article 63 - Existing Rights Preserved

LGPD rights do not exclude other protections from the legal system, including international treaties and conventions to which Brazil is a party.

### Article 64 - Sectoral Rights Preserved

LGPD complements existing Brazilian legislation including the Consumer Defense Code and Marco Civil da Internet (Law 12.965/2014).

### Article 65 - Entry into Force

The law entered into force in stages: ANPD provisions (Articles 55-A through 55-L) on December 28, 2018; administrative sanctions (Articles 52-54) on August 1, 2021; and remaining articles on September 18, 2020. The staggered entry into force allowed organizations time to adapt.

Sanctions include: warnings with corrective deadlines; fines up to 2% of revenue in Brazil (capped at R$ 50 million per infraction); daily fines; publication of the infraction; blocking or deletion of affected data; partial suspension of database operations (up to six months, renewable); suspension of processing activities (up to six months, renewable); or partial/total prohibition of data processing activities.","new_string":"### Article 52 - Administrative Sanctions

Processing agents that commit infractions are subject to the following ANPD-applied sanctions: a warning with a corrective deadline; a simple fine of up to 2% of the private legal entity's revenue in Brazil in the most recent fiscal year, excluding taxes, capped at R$ 50 million per infraction; a daily fine subject to the same cap; publication of the infraction after investigation; blocking of the affected personal data until regularization; deletion of the affected personal data; partial suspension of database operations for up to six months (renewable); suspension of processing activities for up to six months (renewable); or partial or total prohibition of data processing activities."},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":366,"cache_read_input_tokens":35717,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":366,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":24,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"}},"requestId":"req_011CYo414zTz5wX3S4L9ZF9a","type":"assistant","uuid":"d09991eb-bf03-4d27-b953-1fc9e03450e4","timestamp":"2026-03-07T04:01:00.297Z"}

title: "LGPD - Lei Geral de Protecao de Dados (Lei 13.709/2018)"
source: "Presidencia da Republica Federativa do Brasil"
year: 2018
type: regulation
jurisdiction: Brazil
regulatory_body: "Autoridade Nacional de Protecao de Dados (ANPD)"
document_id: "LGPD"
